title: Sicily

infobox.value: [Agrigento](/wiki/Province_of_Agrigento "Province of Agrigento"), [Caltanissetta](/wiki/Province_of_Caltanissetta "Province of Caltanissetta"), [Catania](/wiki/Metropolitan_City_of_Catania "Metropolitan City of Catania"), [Enna](/wiki/Province_of_Enna "Province of Enna"), [Messina](/wiki/Metropolitan_City_of_Messina "Metropolitan City of Messina"), [Palermo](/wiki/Metropolitan_City_of_Palermo "Metropolitan City of Palermo"), [Ragusa](/wiki/Province_of_Ragusa "Province of Ragusa"), [Syracuse](/wiki/Province_of_Syracuse "Province of Syracuse"), [Trapani](/wiki/Province_of_Trapani "Province of Trapani")
0.859[[5]](#cite_note-5) very high · [21st of 21](/wiki/List_of_Italian_regions_by_Human_Development_Index "List of Italian regions by Human Development Index")

([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") and [Sicilian](/wiki/Sicilian_language "Sicilian language"): *Sicilia*)[[a]](#cite_note-6), officially the **Sicilian Region**,[[6]](#cite_note-7) is an island in the central [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") and one of the twenty [regions](/wiki/Regions_of_Italy "Regions of Italy") of [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), situated south of the [Italian Peninsula](/wiki/Italian_Peninsula "Italian Peninsula") in [continental Europe](/wiki/Continental_Europe "Continental Europe"). With over 4.7 million inhabitants, including 1.2 million in and around the capital city of [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo"),[[2]](#cite_note-population-2) it is both the largest and most populous island in the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea").

**Sicily** ([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") and [Sicilian](/wiki/Sicilian_language "Sicilian language"): *Sicilia*)[[a]](#cite_note-6), officially the **Sicilian Region**,[[6]](#cite_note-7) is an island in the central [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") and one of the twenty [regions](/wiki/Regions_of_Italy "Regions of Italy") of [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), situated south of the [Italian Peninsula](/wiki/Italian_Peninsula "Italian Peninsula") in [continental Europe](/wiki/Continental_Europe "Continental Europe"). With over 4.7 million inhabitants, including 1.2 million in and around the capital city of [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo"),[[2]](#cite_note-population-2) it is both the largest and most populous island in the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea").

Sicily's namesake is the [Sicels](/wiki/Sicels "Sicels"), who inhabited the eastern part of the island during the [Iron Age](/wiki/Iron_Age "Iron Age"). Sicily has a rich and unique culture in [arts](#Art_and_architecture), [music](/wiki/Music_of_Sicily "Music of Sicily"), [literature](#Literature), [cuisine](/wiki/Sicilian_cuisine "Sicilian cuisine"), and [architecture](/wiki/Sicilian_Baroque "Sicilian Baroque"). Its most prominent landmark is [Mount Etna](/wiki/Mount_Etna "Mount Etna"), the tallest active [volcano](/wiki/Volcano "Volcano") in Europe, and one of the most active in the world, currently 3,403 m (11,165 ft) high. The island has a typical [Mediterranean climate](/wiki/Mediterranean_climate "Mediterranean climate"). It is separated from [Calabria](/wiki/Calabria "Calabria") by the [Strait of Messina](/wiki/Strait_of_Messina "Strait of Messina"). It is one of the five [Italian autonomous regions](/wiki/Regions_of_Italy#Autonomous_regions_with_special_statute "Regions of Italy") and is considered part of [southern Italy](/wiki/Southern_Italy "Southern Italy").

The earliest [archaeological record](/wiki/Archaeological_record "Archaeological record") of human activity on the island dates to around 14,000 BC. By around 750 BC, Sicily had three [Phoenician](/wiki/Phoenicia "Phoenicia") and around twelve [Greek colonies](/wiki/Greeks_in_Italy "Greeks in Italy") along its coasts, becoming one of the centers of [Magna Graecia](/wiki/Magna_Graecia "Magna Graecia"). The [Sicilian Wars](/wiki/Sicilian_Wars "Sicilian Wars") of 580–265 BC were fought between the Carthaginians and Greeks, and the [Punic Wars](/wiki/Punic_Wars "Punic Wars") of 264–146 BC were fought between Rome and Carthage. The Roman province of [Sicilia](/wiki/Sicilia_(Roman_province) "Sicilia (Roman province)") ended with the fall of the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") in the 5th century AD. Sicily was ruled during the [Early Middle Ages](/wiki/Early_Middle_Ages "Early Middle Ages") by the [Vandals](/wiki/Vandals "Vandals"), the [Ostrogoths](/wiki/Ostrogoths "Ostrogoths"), the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire"), and the [Emirate of Sicily](/wiki/Emirate_of_Sicily "Emirate of Sicily").

The [Norman conquest of southern Italy](/wiki/Norman_conquest_of_southern_Italy "Norman conquest of southern Italy") led to the creation of the [County of Sicily](/wiki/County_of_Sicily "County of Sicily") in 1071, which was succeeded by the [Kingdom of Sicily](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sicily "Kingdom of Sicily") in 1130.[[7]](#cite_note-8)[[8]](#cite_note-9) Following the [Sicilian Vespers](/wiki/Sicilian_Vespers "Sicilian Vespers") in 1282, Sicily was ruled by the [Crown of Aragon](/wiki/Crown_of_Aragon "Crown of Aragon") and then the [Monarchy of Spain](/wiki/Habsburg_Spain "Habsburg Spain"), either in [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union") with the crown or by a [cadet branch](/wiki/Cadet_branch "Cadet branch"), except for a brief period of [Savoy](/wiki/House_of_Savoy "House of Savoy") and then [Habsburg](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg "House of Habsburg") rule in 1713–1735. In 1816, the kingdom unified with the [Kingdom of Naples](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Naples "Kingdom of Naples") to form the [Kingdom of the Two Sicilies](/wiki/Kingdom_of_the_Two_Sicilies "Kingdom of the Two Sicilies"). Following the [Expedition of the Thousand](/wiki/Expedition_of_the_Thousand "Expedition of the Thousand"), an invasion led by [Giuseppe Garibaldi](/wiki/Giuseppe_Garibaldi "Giuseppe Garibaldi"), and a subsequent [plebiscite](/wiki/Referendum "Referendum"), the island became part of the [newly unified](/wiki/Unification_of_Italy "Unification of Italy") [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") in 1860. Sicily was given special status as an [autonomous administrative division](/wiki/Autonomous_administrative_division "Autonomous administrative division") on 15 May 1946, 18 days before the [1946 Italian institutional referendum](/wiki/1946_Italian_institutional_referendum "1946 Italian institutional referendum").

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The name *[Sicilia](/wiki/Sicilia_(Roman_province) "Sicilia (Roman province)")* was given to the [Roman province](/wiki/Roman_province "Roman province") in 241 BC. It is named after the [Sicels](/wiki/Sicels "Sicels"), who inhabited the eastern part of the island during the [Iron Age](/wiki/Iron_Age "Iron Age"). The ancient name of the island is *Trinacria* (Greek [Τρινακρία](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%CE%A4%CF%81%CE%B9%CE%BD%CE%B1%CE%BA%CF%81%CE%AF%CE%B1 "wikt:Τρινακρία") "having three headlands") for its triangular shape, likely a re-interpretation of earlier ([Homeric](/wiki/Homeric_Greek "Homeric Greek")) *[Thrinacia](/wiki/Thrinacia "Thrinacia")*. The Greek name was rendered as *Trīnācrĭa* in [classical Latin](/wiki/Classical_Latin "Classical Latin") ([Virgil](/wiki/Virgil "Virgil"), [Ovid](/wiki/Ovid "Ovid")).[[9]](#cite_note-10)[[10]](#cite_note-11)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Sicily](/wiki/History_of_Sicily "History of Sicily")

### Prehistory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Prehistory")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Orante_1.jpg/250px-Orante_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Orante_1.jpg)

Megaliths of [Argimusco](/wiki/Argimusco "Argimusco"), [Montalbano Elicona](/wiki/Montalbano_Elicona "Montalbano Elicona")

Humans first colonized Sicily towards the end of the [Late Pleistocene](/wiki/Late_Pleistocene "Late Pleistocene"), around 16,000 years ago, by people associated with the [Epigravettian](/wiki/Epigravettian "Epigravettian") culture.[[11]](#cite_note-12)

Discoveries of [dolmens](/wiki/Dolmen "Dolmen") on the island (dating to the second half of the third millennium BC) seem to offer new insights into the culture of primitive Sicily.[[12]](#cite_note-Piccolo-13) The impact of at least two influences is clear: the European one coming from the Northwest, and the Mediterranean influence of an eastern heritage.[[12]](#cite_note-Piccolo-13): 31

### Antiquity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Antiquity")]

Main articles: [Magna Graecia](/wiki/Magna_Graecia "Magna Graecia"), [Ancient Rome](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome"), and [Sicilia (Roman province)](/wiki/Sicilia_(Roman_province) "Sicilia (Roman province)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Murs_Mozia.jpg/250px-Murs_Mozia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Murs_Mozia.jpg)

Ruins of the ancient Phoenician city of [Motya](/wiki/Motya "Motya")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Ginnasium_Solunto.jpg/250px-Ginnasium_Solunto.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ginnasium_Solunto.jpg)

Ruins of the ancient Carthaginian city of [Soluntum](/wiki/Soluntum "Soluntum")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Agrigent_BW_2012-10-07_13-10-58.jpg/250px-Agrigent_BW_2012-10-07_13-10-58.jpg)](/wiki/File:Agrigent_BW_2012-10-07_13-10-58.jpg)

[Temple of Concordia](/wiki/Temple_of_Concordia,_Agrigento "Temple of Concordia, Agrigento"), [Valle dei Templi](/wiki/Valle_dei_Templi "Valle dei Templi"), an [archaeological site](/wiki/Archaeological_site "Archaeological site") in [Agrigento](/wiki/Agrigento "Agrigento"). It is one of the most outstanding examples of ancient Greek art and architecture of [Magna Graecia](/wiki/Magna_Graecia "Magna Graecia").[[13]](#cite_note-14)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Villa_del_Casale_%289528003795%29.jpg/250px-Villa_del_Casale_%289528003795%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Villa_del_Casale_(9528003795).jpg)

[Villa Romana del Casale](/wiki/Villa_Romana_del_Casale "Villa Romana del Casale"), a large and elaborate [Roman villa](/wiki/Roman_villa "Roman villa") or palace located about 3 km from the town of [Piazza Armerina](/wiki/Piazza_Armerina "Piazza Armerina")

The original classical-era inhabitants of Sicily comprised three defined groups of the [ancient peoples of Italy](/wiki/List_of_ancient_peoples_of_Italy "List of ancient peoples of Italy"): the [Sicani](/wiki/Sicani "Sicani"), the [Elymians](/wiki/Elymians "Elymians") and the [Sicels](/wiki/Sicels "Sicels"). The most prominent and by far the earliest of these were the [Sicani](/wiki/Sicani "Sicani"), who ([Thucydides](/wiki/Thucydides "Thucydides") writes) arrived from the [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula") (perhaps [Catalonia](/wiki/Catalonia "Catalonia")).[[14]](#cite_note-15)[[15]](#cite_note-16) Some modern scholars, however, suggest classifying the Sicani as possibly an [Illyrian](/wiki/Illyrians "Illyrians") tribe.[[16]](#cite_note-17) Important historical evidence has been discovered in the form of cave drawings by the Sicani, dated from the end of the [Pleistocene](/wiki/Pleistocene "Pleistocene") epoch around 8000 BC.[[17]](#cite_note-sicanian-18) The [Elymians](/wiki/Elymians "Elymians"), thought to have come from the area of the [Aegean Sea](/wiki/Aegean_Sea "Aegean Sea"), became the next tribe to join the Sicanians on Sicily.[[18]](#cite_note-19)

No evidence survives of warring between tribes, but the Sicanians moved eastwards when the Elymians settled in the northwest corner of the island. The [Sicels](/wiki/Sicels "Sicels") are thought[[17]](#cite_note-sicanian-18) to have originated in [Liguria](/wiki/Liguria "Liguria"); they arrived from mainland Italy in 1200 BC and forced the Sicanians to move back across Sicily and to settle in the middle of the island.[[17]](#cite_note-sicanian-18) Other minor [Italic groups](/wiki/Ancient_peoples_of_Italy "Ancient peoples of Italy") who settled in Sicily included the [Ausones](/wiki/Ausones "Ausones") ([Aeolian Islands](/wiki/Aeolian_Islands "Aeolian Islands"), [Milazzo](/wiki/Milazzo "Milazzo")) and the [Morgetes](/wiki/Morgetes "Morgetes") of [Morgantina](/wiki/Morgantina "Morgantina").

The [Phoenician](/wiki/Phoenicia "Phoenicia") settlements in the western part of the island predate the arrival of [Greek](/wiki/Greek_people "Greek people") colonists.[[19]](#cite_note-catholi-20) From about 750 BC, the Greeks began to live in Sicily ([Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): Σικελία – *Sikelia*), establishing many significant settlements. The most important colony was in [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_Sicily "Syracuse, Sicily"); others grew up at [Akragas](/wiki/Agrigentum "Agrigentum"), [Selinunte](/wiki/Selinunte "Selinunte"), [Gela](/wiki/Gela "Gela"), [Himera](/wiki/Himera "Himera") and [Zancle](/wiki/Messina "Messina").[[20]](#cite_note-21) The native Sicani and Sicel peoples became [absorbed](/wiki/Cultural_assimilation "Cultural assimilation") into the [Hellenic culture](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") with relative ease, and the area became part of [Magna Graecia](/wiki/Magna_Graecia "Magna Graecia") along with the coasts of the [south of the Italian peninsula](/wiki/Southern_Italy "Southern Italy"), which the Greeks had also colonised. Sicily had fertile soils, and the successful introduction of [olives](/wiki/Olive "Olive") and [grape vines](/wiki/Grape_vine "Grape vine") fostered profitable trading.[[21]](#cite_note-knowital-22) [Greek culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Greece "Culture of Greece") significantly centered around [Greek religion](/wiki/Religion_in_ancient_Greece "Religion in ancient Greece"), and the settlers built many [temples](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_temple "Ancient Greek temple") throughout Sicily, including several in the *Valley of the Temples* at [Agrigento](/wiki/Agrigento "Agrigento").[[22]](#cite_note-23)

Politics on the island became intertwined with those of Greece; [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_Sicily "Syracuse, Sicily") became desired by the [Athenians](/wiki/Athenian "Athenian") who set out on the [Sicilian Expedition](/wiki/Sicilian_Expedition "Sicilian Expedition") (415–413 BC) during the [Peloponnesian War](/wiki/Peloponnesian_War "Peloponnesian War"). Syracuse gained [Sparta](/wiki/Sparta "Sparta") and [Corinth](/wiki/Ancient_Corinth "Ancient Corinth") as allies and, as a result, defeated the Athenian expedition. The victors destroyed the Athenian army and their ships, selling most of the survivors into [slavery](/wiki/Slavery_in_ancient_Greece "Slavery in ancient Greece").[[23]](#cite_note-24)

The Greek kingdom of Syracuse controlled most of eastern Sicily while [Carthage](/wiki/Carthage "Carthage") controlled the western side.[[24]](#cite_note-25) The two cultures began to clash, leading to the [Greek-Punic wars](/wiki/Greek-Punic_wars "Greek-Punic wars") (between 580 and 265 BC). The Greek states had begun to make peace with the [Roman Republic](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic") in 205 BC,[[25]](#cite_note-archeologiaviva-26) before the Romans sought to [annex](/wiki/Annexation "Annexation") Sicily as their republic's first [province](/wiki/Roman_province "Roman province"). Rome attacked Carthage's holdings in Sicily in the [First Punic War](/wiki/First_Punic_War "First Punic War") (264 to 241 BC) and won, making Sicily–with the exception of Syracuse–the first Roman province outside of the [Italian Peninsula](/wiki/Italian_Peninsula "Italian Peninsula") by 242 BC.[[26]](#cite_note-27)

In the [Second Punic War](/wiki/Second_Punic_War "Second Punic War") (218 to 201 BC), the Carthaginians attempted to recapture Sicily. Some of the Greek cities on the island who were loyal to Rome switched sides to help the Carthaginians, prompting a Roman military response. [Archimedes](/wiki/Archimedes "Archimedes"), who lived in Syracuse, helped defend his city from Roman invasion; Roman troops killed him after they captured Syracuse in 212 BC.[[27]](#cite_note-28) The Carthaginian attempt failed, and Rome became more unrelenting in its annihilation of the invaders; [Roman consul](/wiki/Roman_consul "Roman consul") [M. Valerian](/wiki/Marcus_Valerius_Laevinus "Marcus Valerius Laevinus") told the [Roman Senate](/wiki/Roman_Senate "Roman Senate") in 210 BC that "no Carthaginian remains in Sicily".[[28]](#cite_note-29)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Agriacastle.jpg/250px-Agriacastle.jpg)](/wiki/File:Agriacastle.jpg)

A 10th century castle built by the [Muslims in Sicily](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_Sicily "Muslim conquest of Sicily") on the mountain top of the mountain in Agira in central Sicily.

As the Roman Republic's [granary](/wiki/Granary "Granary"), Sicily ranked as an important province, divided into two [quaestorships](/wiki/Quaestor "Quaestor"): Syracuse to the east and [Lilybaeum](/wiki/Lilybaeum "Lilybaeum") to the west.[[19]](#cite_note-catholi-20) Roman rule introduced the [Latin language](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language") to the island, which underwent a slow process of [latinisation](/wiki/Latinisation_of_Greek "Latinisation of Greek") but Sicilian culture remained largely Greek and the Greek language did not become extinct on the island, facilitating its [re-hellenisation](/wiki/Hellenization "Hellenization") much later under the Byzantines.[[19]](#cite_note-catholi-20) The prosperity of the island went into sharp decline during the governorship of [Verres](/wiki/Verres "Verres") (73 to 71 BC). In 70 BC, the noted statesman [Cicero](/wiki/Cicero "Cicero") condemned the misgovernment of Verres in his oration *[In Verrem](/wiki/In_Verrem "In Verrem")*.[[29]](#cite_note-30)

Various groups used the island as a power base at different times: slave insurgents occupied it during the [First](/wiki/First_Servile_War "First Servile War") (135−132 BC) and [Second](/wiki/Second_Servile_War "Second Servile War") (104−100 BC) [Servile Wars](/wiki/Servile_Wars "Servile Wars"). [Sextus Pompey](/wiki/Sextus_Pompey "Sextus Pompey") had his headquarters there during the [Sicilian revolt](/wiki/War_between_Sextus_Pompey_and_the_Second_Triumvirate "War between Sextus Pompey and the Second Triumvirate") of 44 to 36 BC. Christianity first appeared in Sicily during the years following AD 200; between this time and AD 313, when Emperor [Constantine the Great](/wiki/Constantine_I "Constantine I") lifted the prohibition on Christianity, a significant number of [Sicilians](/wiki/Sicilians "Sicilians") had become [martyrs](/wiki/Martyr "Martyr"), including [Agatha](/wiki/Agatha_of_Sicily "Agatha of Sicily"), [Christina](/wiki/Saint_Christina_of_Bolsena "Saint Christina of Bolsena"), [Lucy](/wiki/Saint_Lucy "Saint Lucy"), and [Euplius](/wiki/Euplius "Euplius").[[30]](#cite_note-earlymediev-31) Sicily remained a Roman province for around 700 years.[[30]](#cite_note-earlymediev-31)

### Roman and Germanic viceregal rule (469–535)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Roman and Germanic viceregal rule (469–535)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Belisarius_plastered.jpg/250px-Belisarius_plastered.jpg)](/wiki/File:Belisarius_plastered.jpg)

[Belisarius](/wiki/Belisarius "Belisarius") in [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_Sicily "Syracuse, Sicily")

The [Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Western_Roman_Empire "Western Roman Empire") began falling apart after the invasion of [Vandals](/wiki/Vandals "Vandals"), Alans, and Sueves [across the Rhine](/wiki/Crossing_of_the_Rhine "Crossing of the Rhine") on the last day of 406. Eventually the Vandals, after roaming about western and southern [Hispania](/wiki/Hispania "Hispania") (present-day [Iberia](/wiki/Iberia "Iberia")) for 20 years, moved to North Africa in 429 and occupied Carthage in 439. The Franks moved south from present-day Belgium. The Visigoths moved west and eventually settled in Aquitaine in 418; the Burgundians were settled in present-day Savoy in 443.

The Vandals found themselves in a position to threaten Sicily – only 100 miles away from their North African bases.[[31]](#cite_note-jpriv-32) After taking Carthage, the Vandals, personally led by King [Gaiseric](/wiki/Gaiseric "Gaiseric"), laid siege to Palermo in 440 as the opening act in an attempt to wrest the island from Roman rule.[[32]](#cite_note-33) The Vandals made another attempt to take the island one year after the 455 sack of Rome, at Agrigento, but were defeated decisively by [Ricimir](/wiki/Ricimer "Ricimer") in a [naval victory off Corsica](/wiki/Battle_of_Corsica "Battle of Corsica") in 456.[[33]](#cite_note-34) The island remained under Roman rule until 469. The Vandals lost possession of the island 8 years later in 477 to the [East Germanic tribe](/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples") of the [Ostrogoths](/wiki/Ostrogoths "Ostrogoths"), who then controlled Italy and Dalmatia.[[31]](#cite_note-jpriv-32) The island was returned to the Ostrogoths by payment of tribute to their king [Odoacer](/wiki/Odoacer "Odoacer"). He ruled Italy from 476 to 488 in the name of the [Byzantine (Eastern Roman)](/wiki/Byzantine_(Eastern_Roman)_Empire "Byzantine (Eastern Roman) Empire") Emperor. The Vandals kept a toehold in [Lilybaeum](/wiki/Lilybaeum "Lilybaeum"), a port on the west coast. They lost this in 491 after making one last attempt to conquer the island from this port.[[34]](#cite_note-35) The Ostrogothic conquest of Sicily (and of Italy as a whole) under [Theodoric the Great](/wiki/Theodoric_the_Great "Theodoric the Great") began in 488. The Byzantine Emperor [Zeno](/wiki/Zeno_(emperor) "Zeno (emperor)") had appointed Theodoric as a military commander in Italy. The Goths were Germanic, but Theodoric fostered Roman culture and government and allowed freedom of religion.[[35]](#cite_note-36) In 461 from the age of seven or eight until 17 or 18 Theodoric had become a Byzantine hostage; he resided in the great palace of Constantinople, was favored by Emperor [Leo I](/wiki/Leo_I_(emperor) "Leo I (emperor)") (r. 457–474) and learned to read, write and do arithmetic.[[36]](#cite_note-37)

### Byzantine period (535–827)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Byzantine period (535–827)")]

Further information: [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/The_Byzantines_under_Georgios_Maniakes_land_at_Sicily_and_defeat_the_Arabs.jpg/250px-The_Byzantines_under_Georgios_Maniakes_land_at_Sicily_and_defeat_the_Arabs.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Byzantines_under_Georgios_Maniakes_land_at_Sicily_and_defeat_the_Arabs.jpg)

Image depicting the [Byzantine army](/wiki/Byzantine_army "Byzantine army") landing in Sicily commanded by [George Maniaces](/wiki/George_Maniaces "George Maniaces")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/MadridSkylitzesFol100vDetail.jpg/250px-MadridSkylitzesFol100vDetail.jpg)](/wiki/File:MadridSkylitzesFol100vDetail.jpg)

The [Saracen](/wiki/Saracen "Saracen") conquest of the Byzantine stronghold Syracuse, [Siege of Syracuse (877–878)](/wiki/Siege_of_Syracuse_(877%E2%80%93878) "Siege of Syracuse (877–878)")

After taking areas occupied by the Vandals in North Africa, [Justinian I](/wiki/Justinian_I "Justinian I") retook Italy as an ambitious attempt to recover the lost provinces in the West. The re-conquests marked an end to over 150 years of accommodating policies with tribal invaders. His first target was Sicily, leading to the [Gothic War (535–554)](/wiki/Gothic_War_(535%E2%80%93554) "Gothic War (535–554)") between the Ostrogoths and the Eastern Roman Empire, also known as the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire"). Justinian's general [Belisarius](/wiki/Belisarius "Belisarius") was assigned to the military task.[[37]](#cite_note-38) Sicily was used as a base for the Byzantines to conquer the rest of Italy, including [Naples](/wiki/Naples "Naples"), Rome, and [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan"). It took five years before the Ostrogoth capital [Ravenna](/wiki/Ravenna "Ravenna") fell in 540.[[38]](#cite_note-hisnet-39) However, the new Ostrogoth king [Totila](/wiki/Totila "Totila") counterattacked, moving down the Italian peninsula, plundering and conquering Sicily in 550. Totila was defeated and killed in the [Battle of Taginae](/wiki/Battle_of_Taginae "Battle of Taginae") by Byzantine general [Narses](/wiki/Narses "Narses") in 552 but Italy was in ruins.[[38]](#cite_note-hisnet-39)

At the time of the reconquest Greek was still the predominant language spoken on the island. Sicily was invaded by the [Arab forces](/wiki/Rashidun_army "Rashidun army") of [Caliph Uthman](/wiki/Uthman_Ibn_Affan "Uthman Ibn Affan") in 652, but the Arabs failed to make permanent gains. They returned to Syria with their booty.[[39]](#cite_note-40) Raids seeking loot continued until the mid-8th century.[[40]](#cite_note-41)

The Eastern Roman Emperor [Constans II](/wiki/Constans_II_(Byzantine_Empire) "Constans II (Byzantine Empire)") moved from [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople") to [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_Sicily "Syracuse, Sicily") in 660. The following year he launched an assault from Sicily against the [Lombard](/wiki/Lombardy "Lombardy") [Duchy of Benevento](/wiki/Duchy_of_Benevento "Duchy of Benevento"), which occupied most of southern Italy.[[41]](#cite_note-travsyrac-42) Rumors that the capital of the empire was to be moved to Syracuse probably cost Constans his life, as he was assassinated in 668.[[41]](#cite_note-travsyrac-42) His son [Constantine IV](/wiki/Constantine_IV "Constantine IV") succeeded him. A brief usurpation in Sicily by [Mezezius](/wiki/Mezezius "Mezezius") was quickly suppressed by this emperor. Contemporary accounts report that the Greek language was widely spoken on the island during this period.[[42]](#cite_note-43) In 740 Emperor [Leo III the Isaurian](/wiki/Leo_III_the_Isaurian "Leo III the Isaurian") transferred Sicily from the jurisdiction of the church of Rome to that of Constantinople, placing the island within the eastern branch of the Church.[[43]](#cite_note-44)

In 826 [Euphemius](/wiki/Euphemius_(Sicily) "Euphemius (Sicily)"), the Byzantine commander in Sicily, having apparently killed his wife, forced a nun to marry him. Emperor [Michael II](/wiki/Michael_II "Michael II") caught wind of the matter and ordered general Constantine to end the marriage and cut off Euphemius' head. Euphemius rose up, killed Constantine, and then occupied Syracuse; he, in turn, was defeated and driven out to North Africa.[[44]](#cite_note-stan-45) He offered the rule of Sicily to [Ziyadat Allah](/wiki/Ziyadat_Allah_I_of_Aghlabids "Ziyadat Allah I of Aghlabids"), the [Aghlabid](/wiki/Aghlabid "Aghlabid") Emir of [Tunisia](/wiki/Tunisia "Tunisia"), in return for a position as a general and a place of safety. A [Muslim army](/wiki/Early_Muslim_conquests "Early Muslim conquests") was then sent to the island consisting of [Arabs](/wiki/Arab "Arab"), [Berbers](/wiki/Berber_people "Berber people"), [Cretans](/wiki/Cretans "Cretans"), and [Persians](/wiki/Persian_people "Persian people").[[44]](#cite_note-stan-45)

The [Muslim conquest of Sicily](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_Sicily "Muslim conquest of Sicily") was a see-saw affair and met with fierce resistance. It took over a century for Byzantine Sicily to be conquered; the largest city, Syracuse, held out until 878 and the Greek city of [Taormina](/wiki/Taormina "Taormina") fell in 902. It was not until 965 that all of Sicily was conquered by the Arabs.[[44]](#cite_note-stan-45) In the 11th-century Byzantine armies carried out a partial reconquest of the island under [George Maniakes](/wiki/George_Maniakes "George Maniakes"), but it was their [Norman](/wiki/Italo-Norman "Italo-Norman") mercenaries who would eventually complete the island's reconquest at the end of the century.

### Arab period (827–1091)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Arab period (827–1091)")]

Main article: [Emirate of Sicily](/wiki/Emirate_of_Sicily "Emirate of Sicily")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Cuba_muqarna.jpeg/250px-Cuba_muqarna.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Cuba_muqarna.jpeg)

[Arabesque](/wiki/Arabesque "Arabesque") on a wall in the [Cuba Palace](/wiki/Cuba,_Palermo "Cuba, Palermo") in Palermo

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/HorologiumPanormi.jpg/250px-HorologiumPanormi.jpg)](/wiki/File:HorologiumPanormi.jpg)

Trilingual sign from the [Palazzo dei Normanni](/wiki/Palazzo_dei_Normanni "Palazzo dei Normanni") in [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo"), written in [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"), [Byzantine Greek](/wiki/Medieval_Greek "Medieval Greek") and [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic")

The language spoken in Sicily under Arab rule was [Siculo-Arabic](/wiki/Siculo-Arabic "Siculo-Arabic") and [Arabic influence](/wiki/Influence_of_Arabic_on_other_languages "Influence of Arabic on other languages") is present in some Sicilian words today. Although long extinct in Sicily, the language has developed into what is now the [Maltese language](/wiki/Maltese_language "Maltese language") on the islands of [Malta](/wiki/Malta "Malta") today.[[45]](#cite_note-46)

A description of [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo") was given by [Ibn Hawqal](/wiki/Ibn_Hawqal "Ibn Hawqal"), an [Arab merchant](/wiki/History_of_Islamic_economics "History of Islamic economics") who visited Sicily in 950. A walled suburb, called the Al-Kasr (the palace), is the centre of Palermo to this day, with the great Friday mosque on the site of the later Roman cathedral. The suburb of al-Khalisa (modern [Kalsa](/wiki/Kalsa "Kalsa")) contained the [Sultan](/wiki/Sultan "Sultan")'s palace, baths, a mosque, government offices, and a private prison. [Ibn Hawqal](/wiki/Ibn_Hawqal "Ibn Hawqal") estimated there were 7,000 butchers trading in 150 shops. The Muslim rule introduced lemons, oranges, pistachios,[[46]](#cite_note-47) and sugar cane, as well as cotton and mulberries for sericulture, and introduced the [Qanat](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Qanat_di_Palermo "it:Qanat di Palermo") to improve irrigation systems for agriculture.[[47]](#cite_note-Metcalfe2014-48) Around 1050, the western half of Sicily was ethnically and culturally distinct from central and eastern Sicily. During this time, there was also a small Jewish presence in Sicily, evidence seen in the catacombs discovered on the island.[[48]](#cite_note-49)[[49]](#cite_note-50)

Palermo was initially ruled by the [Aghlabids](/wiki/Aghlabids "Aghlabids"); later it was the centre of the Emirate of Sicily, which was under the nominal suzerainty of the [Fatimid Caliphate](/wiki/Fatimid_Caliphate "Fatimid Caliphate"). Muslim sovereignty was never absolute across the island, and the creation of three subdivisions served to distinguish different approaches to government. Under the Arab rule the island was divided in [three administrative regions](/wiki/Three_valli_of_Sicily "Three valli of Sicily"), or "vals", roughly corresponding to the three "points" of Sicily: [Val di Mazara](/wiki/Val_di_Mazara "Val di Mazara") in the west; [Val Demone](/wiki/Val_Demone "Val Demone") in the northeast; and [Val di Noto](/wiki/Val_di_Noto "Val di Noto") in the southeast. As [dhimmis](/wiki/Dhimmi "Dhimmi"), that is as members of a protected class of approved monotheists, the [Eastern Orthodox Christians](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") were allowed [freedom of religion](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion "Freedom of religion"), but had to pay a tax, the [jizya](/wiki/Jizya "Jizya") (in lieu of the obligatory alms tax, the [zakat](/wiki/Zakat "Zakat"), paid by Muslims), and were restricted from active participation in public affairs. Western Sicily was more [Islamized](/wiki/Islamization "Islamization") and heavily populated by Arabs, allowing for full and direct administration; by contrast, the northeast region of [Val Demone](/wiki/Val_Demone "Val Demone") remained majority Christian and often resistant to Muslim rule, prompting a focus on tax collection and maintaining public order, as a result, revolts by Byzantine Sicilians continuously occurred in the east where Greek-speaking Christians predominated. Parts of the island were re-occupied before revolts were quashed. By the 11th century, the [Emirate of Sicily](/wiki/Emirate_of_Sicily "Emirate of Sicily") began to fragment as intra-dynastic quarreling fractured the Muslim government.[[44]](#cite_note-stan-45)

### Norman Sicily (1038–1198)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Norman Sicily (1038–1198)")]

See also: [Norman conquest of southern Italy](/wiki/Norman_conquest_of_southern_Italy "Norman conquest of southern Italy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Kingdom_of_Sicily_1154.svg/250px-Kingdom_of_Sicily_1154.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Kingdom_of_Sicily_1154.svg)

The Norman [Kingdom of Sicily](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sicily "Kingdom of Sicily") (in green) in 1154, upon the death of [Roger II](/wiki/Roger_II "Roger II")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Cappella_Palatina_Kuppel.jpg/250px-Cappella_Palatina_Kuppel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cappella_Palatina_Kuppel.jpg)

[Cappella Palatina](/wiki/Cappella_Palatina "Cappella Palatina"), [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo")

In 1038, seventy years after losing their last cities in Sicily, the Byzantines under the Greek general [George Maniakes](/wiki/George_Maniakes "George Maniakes") invaded the island together with their [Varangian](/wiki/Varangian_guard "Varangian guard") and [Norman](/wiki/Normans "Normans") mercenaries. Maniakes was killed in a Byzantine civil war in 1043 before completing a reconquest and the Byzantines withdrew. Later the Normans invaded in 1061 and after taking [Apulia](/wiki/Apulia "Apulia") and [Calabria](/wiki/Calabria "Calabria"), [Roger I](/wiki/Roger_I_of_Sicily "Roger I of Sicily") occupied [Messina](/wiki/Messina "Messina") with an army of 700 knights.[[44]](#cite_note-stan-45)[[50]](#cite_note-Boise_State_University_Sicily_under_the_ormans-51) In 1068, Roger I was victorious at [Misilmeri](/wiki/Battle_of_Misilmeri "Battle of Misilmeri"). Most crucial was the siege of Palermo, whose fall in 1071 eventually resulted in all Sicily coming under Norman control.[[51]](#cite_note-initalymag-52) The conquest was completed in 1091 when they captured [Noto](/wiki/Noto "Noto") the last Arab stronghold. Palermo continued to be the capital under the [Normans](/wiki/Normans "Normans"). The Normans formed a small but violent ruling class. They destroyed many of the Arab towns in Sicily, and very few physical remains survive from the Arab era.[[44]](#cite_note-stan-45)

The Norman [Hauteville family](/wiki/Hauteville_family "Hauteville family") appreciated and admired the rich and layered culture in which they now found themselves. They also introduced into Sicily their own culture, customs, and politics from [Normandy](/wiki/Normandy "Normandy"). Many Normans in Sicily adopted the habits and comportment of Muslim rulers and their Byzantine subjects in dress, language, literature, even to the extent of having palace [eunuchs](/wiki/Eunuch "Eunuch") and, according to some accounts, a harem.[[52]](#cite_note-53)[[53]](#cite_note-54)

While Roger I died in 1101, his wife [Adelaide](/wiki/Adelaide_del_Vasto "Adelaide del Vasto") ruled until 1112 when their son [Roger II of Sicily](/wiki/Roger_II_of_Sicily "Roger II of Sicily") came of age.[[50]](#cite_note-Boise_State_University_Sicily_under_the_ormans-51) Having succeeded his brother [Simon](/wiki/Simon_of_Sicily "Simon of Sicily") as Count of Sicily, Roger II was ultimately able to raise the status of the island to a kingdom in 1130, along with his other holdings, which included the [Maltese Islands](/wiki/Maltese_Islands "Maltese Islands") and the Duchies of [Apulia](/wiki/Duchy_of_Apulia "Duchy of Apulia") and [Calabria](/wiki/Duchy_of_Calabria "Duchy of Calabria").[[51]](#cite_note-initalymag-52)[[54]](#cite_note-55) Roger II appointed the powerful Greek [George of Antioch](/wiki/George_of_Antioch "George of Antioch") to be his "emir of emirs" and continued the syncretism of his father. During this period, the Kingdom of Sicily was prosperous and politically powerful, becoming one of the wealthiest states in all of Europe—even wealthier than the [Kingdom of England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England").[[55]](#cite_note-56)

The court of Roger II became the most luminous centre of culture in the Mediterranean, both from Europe and the Middle East, like the multi-ethnic [Caliphate of Córdoba](/wiki/Caliphate_of_C%C3%B3rdoba "Caliphate of Córdoba"), then only just eclipsed. This attracted scholars, scientists, poets, artists, and artisans of all kinds. Laws were issued in the language of the community to whom they were addressed in Norman Sicily, at the time when the culture was still heavily Arab and Greek.[[56]](#cite_note-Advanced_Studies_in_Cultural_History-57)[[57]](#cite_note-Loud,_G._A._2007_494-58) Governance was by rule of law which promoted justice. Muslims, Jews, [Byzantine Greeks](/wiki/Byzantine_Greeks "Byzantine Greeks"), Lombards, and Normans worked together fairly amicably. During this time many extraordinary buildings were constructed.[[56]](#cite_note-Advanced_Studies_in_Cultural_History-57)

However this situation changed as the Normans imported immigrants from [Normandy](/wiki/Normandy "Normandy"), [England](/wiki/England "England"), Lombardy, Piedmont, Provence and [Campania](/wiki/Campania "Campania") to secure the island. Linguistically, the island shifted from being one-third Greek- and two-thirds Arabic-speaking at the time of the Norman conquest to becoming fully [Latinised](/wiki/Linguistic_Latinisation "Linguistic Latinisation").[[57]](#cite_note-Loud,_G._A._2007_494-58) In terms of religion the island became completely Roman Catholic (bearing in mind that until 1054 the Churches owing allegiance to the Pope and the Patriarch of Constantinople belonged to one Church); Sicily before the Norman conquest was under the Eastern Orthodox Patriarch.[[58]](#cite_note-59) After Pope Innocent III made him Papal Legate in 1098, Roger I created several Catholic bishoprics while still allowing the construction of 12 Greek-speaking monasteries (the Greek language, monasteries, and 1500 parishes continued to exist until the adherents of the Greek Rite were forced in 1585 to convert to Catholicism or leave; a small pocket of Greek-speakers still live in Messina).

### Kingdom of Sicily (1198–1860)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Kingdom of Sicily (1198–1860)")]

Main articles: [Kingdom of Sicily](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sicily "Kingdom of Sicily") and [List of monarchs of Sicily](/wiki/List_of_monarchs_of_Sicily "List of monarchs of Sicily")

After a century, the Norman [Hauteville](/wiki/Hauteville_family "Hauteville family") dynasty died out; the last direct descendant and heir of Roger II, [Constance](/wiki/Constance_I_of_Sicily "Constance I of Sicily"), married [Emperor Henry VI](/wiki/Henry_VI,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Henry VI, Holy Roman Emperor").[[59]](#cite_note-dieli-60) This eventually led to the crown of Sicily being passed to the [Hohenstaufen](/wiki/Hohenstaufen "Hohenstaufen") dynasty, who were Germans from [Swabia](/wiki/Swabia "Swabia"). The last of the Hohenstaufens, [Frederick II](/wiki/Frederick_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Frederick II, Holy Roman Emperor"), the only son of [Constance](/wiki/Constance_I_of_Sicily "Constance I of Sicily"), was one of the greatest and most cultured men of the Middle Ages. His mother's will had asked [Pope Innocent III](/wiki/Pope_Innocent_III "Pope Innocent III") to undertake the guardianship of her son. Frederick was four when at [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo"), he was crowned [King of Sicily](/wiki/King_of_Sicily "King of Sicily") in 1198. Frederick received no systematic education and was allowed to run free in the streets of [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo"). There he picked up the many languages he heard spoken, such as Arabic and Greek, and learned some of the lore of the Jewish community. At age twelve, he dismissed Innocent's deputy regent and took over the government; at fifteen he married [Constance of Aragon](/wiki/Constance_of_Aragon,_Holy_Roman_Empress "Constance of Aragon, Holy Roman Empress"), and began his reclamation of the imperial crown. Subsequently, due to Muslim rebellions, Frederick II destroyed the remaining Muslim presence in Sicily, estimated at 60,000 people, moving all to the city of Lucera in Apulia between 1221 and 1226.[[60]](#cite_note-61)

Conflict between the Hohenstaufen house and the [Papacy](/wiki/Papacy "Papacy") led, in 1266, to [Pope Innocent IV](/wiki/Pope_Innocent_IV "Pope Innocent IV") crowning the [French prince](/wiki/Capetian_House_of_Anjou "Capetian House of Anjou") [Charles](/wiki/Charles_I_of_Naples "Charles I of Naples"), [count of Anjou](/wiki/Count_of_Anjou "Count of Anjou") and [Provence](/wiki/County_of_Provence "County of Provence"), as the king of both Sicily and Naples.[[59]](#cite_note-dieli-60)

Strong opposition to French officialdom due to mistreatment and taxation saw the local peoples of Sicily rise up, leading in 1282 to an [insurrection](/wiki/Insurrection "Insurrection") known as the [War of the Sicilian Vespers](/wiki/War_of_the_Sicilian_Vespers "War of the Sicilian Vespers"), which eventually saw almost the entire French population on the island killed.[[59]](#cite_note-dieli-60) During the war, the Sicilians turned to [Peter III of Aragon](/wiki/Peter_III_of_Aragon "Peter III of Aragon"), son-in-law of the last Hohenstaufen king, for support after being rejected by the Pope. Peter gained control of Sicily from the French, who, however, retained control of the [Kingdom of Naples](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Naples "Kingdom of Naples"). A crusade was launched in August 1283 against Peter III and the Crown of Aragon by [Pope Martin IV](/wiki/Pope_Martin_IV "Pope Martin IV") (a pope from [Île-de-France](/wiki/%C3%8Ele-de-France "Île-de-France")), but it failed. The wars continued until the [peace of Caltabellotta](/wiki/Peace_of_Caltabellotta "Peace of Caltabellotta") in 1302, which saw Peter's son [Frederick III](/wiki/Frederick_III_of_Sicily "Frederick III of Sicily") recognized as the king of the Isle of Sicily, while [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_Naples "Charles II of Naples") was recognized as the king of Naples by [Pope Boniface VIII](/wiki/Pope_Boniface_VIII "Pope Boniface VIII").[[59]](#cite_note-dieli-60) Sicily was ruled as an independent kingdom by relatives of the kings of Aragon until 1409 and then as part of the [Crown of Aragon](/wiki/Crown_of_Aragon "Crown of Aragon").[[21]](#cite_note-knowital-22)

In October 1347, in Messina, Sicily, the [Black Death](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death") first arrived in Europe.[[61]](#cite_note-62)

Between the 15th and 18th centuries, waves of [Greeks](/wiki/Greeks "Greeks") from the [Peloponnese](/wiki/Peloponnese "Peloponnese") (such as the [Maniots](/wiki/Maniots "Maniots")) and [Arvanites](/wiki/Arvanites "Arvanites") migrated to Sicily in large numbers to escape persecution after the [Ottoman conquest of the Peloponnese](/wiki/Ottoman_conquest_of_the_Balkans "Ottoman conquest of the Balkans"). They brought with them [Eastern Orthodoxy](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy") as well as the [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") and [Arvanitika](/wiki/Arvanitika "Arvanitika") languages, once again adding onto the extensive [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine "Byzantine")/[Greek](/wiki/Greek_Culture "Greek Culture") influence.

The onset of the [Spanish Inquisition](/wiki/Spanish_Inquisition "Spanish Inquisition") in 1492 led to [Ferdinand II](/wiki/Ferdinand_II_of_Aragon "Ferdinand II of Aragon") decreeing the expulsion of all Jews from Sicily.[[59]](#cite_note-dieli-60) The eastern part of the island was hit by destructive earthquakes in 1542 and 1693. Just a few years before the latter earthquake, the island was struck by a [plague](/wiki/Plague_(disease) "Plague (disease)").[[59]](#cite_note-dieli-60) The [earthquake in 1693](/wiki/1693_Sicily_earthquake "1693 Sicily earthquake") took an estimated 60,000 lives.[[62]](#cite_note-63) There were revolts during the 17th century, but these were quelled with force, especially the revolts of Palermo and Messina.[[21]](#cite_note-knowital-22) [North African](/wiki/Barbary_pirates "Barbary pirates") [slave raids](/wiki/Barbary_slave_trade "Barbary slave trade") discouraged settlement along the coast until the 19th century.[[63]](#cite_note-64)[[64]](#cite_note-65) The [Treaty of Utrecht](/wiki/Treaty_of_Utrecht "Treaty of Utrecht") in 1713 saw Sicily assigned to the [House of Savoy](/wiki/House_of_Savoy "House of Savoy"); however, this period of rule lasted only seven years, as it was exchanged for the island of [Sardinia](/wiki/Sardinia "Sardinia") with [Emperor Charles VI](/wiki/Charles_VI,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles VI, Holy Roman Emperor") of the Austrian [Habsburg Dynasty](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg "House of Habsburg").[[65]](#cite_note-66)

While the Austrians were concerned with the [War of the Polish Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Polish_Succession "War of the Polish Succession"), a [Bourbon](/wiki/House_of_Bourbon "House of Bourbon") prince, [Charles](/wiki/Charles_III_of_Spain "Charles III of Spain") from Spain was able to conquer Sicily and Naples.[[66]](#cite_note-67) At first Sicily was able to remain as an independent kingdom under [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union"), while the Bourbons ruled over both from Naples. However, the advent of [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon_I "Napoleon I")'s [First French Empire](/wiki/First_French_Empire "First French Empire") saw Naples taken at the [Battle of Campo Tenese](/wiki/Battle_of_Campo_Tenese "Battle of Campo Tenese") and Bonapartist [King of Naples](/wiki/King_of_Naples "King of Naples") was installed. [Ferdinand III](/wiki/Ferdinand_I_of_the_Two_Sicilies "Ferdinand I of the Two Sicilies"), the Bourbon, was forced to retreat to Sicily which he was still in control of with the help of [British naval](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") protection.[[67]](#cite_note-68)

Following this, Sicily joined the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars"), and subsequently the British under [Lord William Bentinck](/wiki/Lord_William_Bentinck "Lord William Bentinck") established a military and diplomatic presence on the island to protect against a French invasion. Sicilian volunteers joined the British military to form the [Royal Sicilian Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Sicilian_Regiment "Royal Sicilian Regiment"), which saw action at the [Battle of Maida](/wiki/Battle_of_Maida "Battle of Maida") and then transferred to [Iberia](/wiki/Peninsular_War "Peninsular War"). After the wars were won, Sicily and Naples formally merged as the [Two Sicilies](/wiki/Two_Sicilies "Two Sicilies") under the Bourbons. Major [revolutionary](/wiki/Revolutionary "Revolutionary") movements occurred in 1820 and 1848 against the Bourbon government with Sicily seeking independence; the second of which, the [1848 revolution](/wiki/Sicilian_revolution_of_independence_of_1848 "Sicilian revolution of independence of 1848") resulted in a short period of independence for Sicily. However, in 1849 the Bourbons retook control of the island and dominated it until 1860.[[68]](#cite_note-69)

### Italian unification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Italian unification")]

See also: [Risorgimento](/wiki/Risorgimento "Risorgimento")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Garibaldi_sbarca_a_Marsala_-_Gerolamo_Induno.jpg/250px-Garibaldi_sbarca_a_Marsala_-_Gerolamo_Induno.jpg)](/wiki/File:Garibaldi_sbarca_a_Marsala_-_Gerolamo_Induno.jpg)

[Giuseppe Garibaldi](/wiki/Giuseppe_Garibaldi "Giuseppe Garibaldi") lands in [Marsala](/wiki/Marsala "Marsala") in 1860, during the [Expedition of the Thousand](/wiki/Expedition_of_the_Thousand "Expedition of the Thousand"), as part of the [Italian unification](/wiki/Italian_unification "Italian unification")

The [Expedition of the Thousand](/wiki/Expedition_of_the_Thousand "Expedition of the Thousand") led by [Giuseppe Garibaldi](/wiki/Giuseppe_Garibaldi "Giuseppe Garibaldi") captured Sicily in 1860, as part of the *[Risorgimento](/wiki/Italian_unification "Italian unification")*.[[69]](#cite_note-modern-70) The conquest started at [Marsala](/wiki/Marsala "Marsala"), and native Sicilians joined him in the capture of the southern Italian peninsula. Garibaldi's march was completed with the [siege of Gaeta](/wiki/Siege_of_Gaeta_(1861) "Siege of Gaeta (1861)"), where the final [Bourbons](/wiki/Bourbons "Bourbons") were expelled and Garibaldi announced his dictatorship in the name of [Victor Emmanuel II](/wiki/Victor_Emanuel_II_of_Italy "Victor Emanuel II of Italy") of [Kingdom of Sardinia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sardinia_(1720%E2%80%931861) "Kingdom of Sardinia (1720–1861)").[[70]](#cite_note-71) Sicily became part of the Kingdom of Sardinia after a referendum in which more than 75% of Sicily voted in favour of the annexation on 21 October 1860 (although not everyone was allowed to vote). As a result of the [proclamation of the Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_Kingdom_of_Italy "Proclamation of the Kingdom of Italy"), Sicily became part of the kingdom on 17 March 1861.

The Sicilian economy (and the wider *[mezzogiorno](/wiki/Mezzogiorno "Mezzogiorno")* economy) remained relatively underdeveloped after the [Italian unification](/wiki/Italian_unification "Italian unification"), in spite of the strong investments made by the [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy") in terms of modern infrastructure, and this caused an unprecedented [wave of emigration](/wiki/Italian_diaspora "Italian diaspora").[[69]](#cite_note-modern-70) In 1894, organisations of workers and peasants known as the *[Fasci Siciliani](/wiki/Fasci_Siciliani "Fasci Siciliani")* protested against the bad social and economic conditions of the island, but they were suppressed in a few days.[[71]](#cite_note-72)[[72]](#cite_note-73)

This period was also characterized by the first contact between the [Sicilian Mafia](/wiki/Sicilian_Mafia "Sicilian Mafia") (the crime syndicate also known as Cosa Nostra) and the Italian government. The Mafia's origins are still uncertain, but it is generally accepted that it emerged in the 18th century initially in the role of private enforcers hired to protect the property of landowners and merchants from the groups of [*briganti*](/wiki/Brigandage "Brigandage") who frequently pillaged the countryside and towns. The battle against the Mafia made by the Kingdom of Italy was controversial and ambiguous. The [Carabinieri](/wiki/Carabinieri "Carabinieri") (the military police of Italy) and sometimes the [Royal Italian Army](/wiki/Royal_Italian_Army "Royal Italian Army") were often involved in fights against the mafia members, but their efforts were frequently useless because of the weakness of the Italian judicial system and cooperation between the mafia and local governments.[[73]](#cite_note-74)

### 20th and 21st centuries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 20th and 21st centuries")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Underwood_%26_Underwood_%C2%A9_1906_No._10495_-_Messina_-_The_once_beautiful_Water-front_after_the_earthquake_dett.jpg/250px-Underwood_%26_Underwood_%C2%A9_1906_No._10495_-_Messina_-_The_once_beautiful_Water-front_after_the_earthquake_dett.jpg)](/wiki/File:Underwood_%26_Underwood_%C2%A9_1906_No._10495_-_Messina_-_The_once_beautiful_Water-front_after_the_earthquake_dett.jpg)

Ruins of [Messina](/wiki/Messina "Messina") after the [1908 earthquake](/wiki/1908_Messina_earthquake "1908 Messina earthquake") and tsunami. Taken at the northern section of the Palazzata in front of the harbour.

The [Messina earthquake](/wiki/1908_Messina_earthquake "1908 Messina earthquake") of 28 December 1908 killed more than 80,000 people.[[74]](#cite_note-75)

In the 1920s, the [fascist](/wiki/Italian_fascism "Italian fascism") regime began taking stronger military action, led by [Cesare Mori](/wiki/Cesare_Mori "Cesare Mori") (nicknamed the "Iron [Prefect](/wiki/Prefect "Prefect")" for his iron-fisted campaigns), against the Sicilian Mafia, the first that ended with considerable success.[[69]](#cite_note-modern-70) An [Allied invasion of Sicily](/wiki/Allied_invasion_of_Sicily "Allied invasion of Sicily") during World War II began on 10 July 1943. In preparation for the invasion, the Allies [revitalised](/wiki/Collaborations_between_the_United_States_government_and_Italian_Mafia "Collaborations between the United States government and Italian Mafia") the Mafia to aid them. The invasion of Sicily contributed to the [25 July crisis](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Fascist_regime_in_Italy "Fall of the Fascist regime in Italy"); in general, the Allied victors were warmly embraced by Sicilians.[[75]](#cite_note-76)

In the aftermath of World War II, Italy [became a republic](/wiki/1946_Italian_institutional_referendum "1946 Italian institutional referendum") in 1946. Under the [Constitution of Italy](/wiki/Constitution_of_Italy "Constitution of Italy"), Sicily is one of five [regions](/wiki/Regions_of_Italy "Regions of Italy") with [autonomy](/wiki/Autonomous_administrative_division "Autonomous administrative division").[[76]](#cite_note-77) Both the partial Italian [land reform](/wiki/Land_reform "Land reform") and special funding from the Italian government's *[Cassa per il Mezzogiorno](/wiki/Cassa_per_il_Mezzogiorno "Cassa per il Mezzogiorno")* (*Fund for the South*) from 1950 to 1984 helped the Sicilian economy. During this period, the economic and social condition of the island was generally improved because of investments in infrastructure (such as [motorways](/wiki/Motorways "Motorways") and [airports](/wiki/Airport "Airport")) and the creation of industrial and commercial areas.[[77]](#cite_note-78) In the 1980s, the Mafia was weakened by another campaign led by magistrates [Giovanni Falcone](/wiki/Giovanni_Falcone "Giovanni Falcone") and [Paolo Borsellino](/wiki/Paolo_Borsellino "Paolo Borsellino").[[78]](#cite_note-79) Between 1990 and 2005, the [unemployment rate](/wiki/Unemployment_rate "Unemployment rate") fell from about 23% to 11%.[[79]](#cite_note-80)[[80]](#cite_note-81)

The Cosa Nostra has traditionally been the most powerful group in Sicily, especially around Palermo.[[81]](#cite_note-82) A police investigation in the summer of 2019 confirmed strong links between the Palermo area [Sicilian Mafia](/wiki/Sicilian_Mafia "Sicilian Mafia") and American organized crime, particularly the [Gambino crime family](/wiki/Gambino_crime_family "Gambino crime family").[[82]](#cite_note-83) According to *[La Repubblica](/wiki/La_Repubblica "La Repubblica")*, "Off they go, through the streets of Passo di Rigano, Boccadifalco, Torretta and at the same time, Brooklyn, Staten Island, [and] New Jersey. Because from Sicily to the US, the old mafia has returned."[[83]](#cite_note-84)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Geography")]

See also: [Geology of Sicily](/wiki/Geology_of_Sicily "Geology of Sicily")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Sicily_Map.png/250px-Sicily_Map.png)](/wiki/File:Sicily_Map.png)

Relief map of Sicily

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Sicily2025OSM.png/250px-Sicily2025OSM.png)](/wiki/File:Sicily2025OSM.png)

Detailed map of Sicily

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Madonie_montagna.jpg/250px-Madonie_montagna.jpg)](/wiki/File:Madonie_montagna.jpg)

[Madonie](/wiki/Madonie "Madonie") mountains in the [Madonie Regional Natural Park](/wiki/Madonie_Regional_Natural_Park "Madonie Regional Natural Park").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Mare_Favignana.JPG/250px-Mare_Favignana.JPG)](/wiki/File:Mare_Favignana.JPG)

Cala Rossa, [island of Favignana](/wiki/Favignana#Island "Favignana"), [Aegadian Islands](/wiki/Aegadian_Islands "Aegadian Islands")

Sicily has a roughly triangular shape, earning it the name *Trinacria*.

To the north-east, it is separated from [Calabria](/wiki/Calabria "Calabria") and the rest of the Italian mainland by the [Strait of Messina](/wiki/Strait_of_Messina "Strait of Messina"), about 3 km (1.9 mi) wide in the north, and about 16 km (9.9 mi) wide in the southern part.[[84]](#cite_note-85) The northern and southern coasts are each about 280 km (170 mi) long measured as a straight line, while the eastern coast measures around 180 km (110 mi); total coast length is [estimated](/wiki/Length_of_coast "Length of coast") at 1,484 km (922 mi). The total area of the island is 25,711 km2 (9,927 sq mi),[[85]](#cite_note-eb-86) while the [Autonomous Region](/wiki/Autonomous_administrative_division "Autonomous administrative division") of Sicily (which includes the smaller surrounding islands of [Lipari](/wiki/Lipari "Lipari"), [Egadi](/wiki/Egadi "Egadi"), [Ustica](/wiki/Ustica "Ustica"), and [Pantelleria](/wiki/Pantelleria "Pantelleria")) has an area of 27,708 km2 (10,698 sq mi).[[86]](#cite_note-87)

The terrain of inland Sicily is mostly hilly and is intensively cultivated wherever possible. Along the northern coast, the [mountain ranges](/wiki/Mountain_range "Mountain range") of [Madonie](/wiki/Madonie "Madonie"), 2,000 m (6,600 ft), [Nebrodi](/wiki/Nebrodi "Nebrodi"), 1,800 m (5,900 ft), and [Peloritani](/wiki/Peloritani "Peloritani"), 1,300 m (4,300 ft), are an extension of the mainland [Apennines](/wiki/Apennines "Apennines"). The cone of [Mount Etna](/wiki/Mount_Etna "Mount Etna") dominates the eastern coast. In the southeast lie the lower [Hyblaean Mountains](/wiki/Hyblaean_Mountains "Hyblaean Mountains"), 1,000 m (3,300 ft).[[87]](#cite_note-frommers-88) The mines of the [Enna](/wiki/Enna "Enna") and [Caltanissetta](/wiki/Caltanissetta "Caltanissetta") districts were part of a leading [sulphur](/wiki/Sulphur "Sulphur")-producing area throughout the 19th century, but have declined since the 1950s.

Sicily and its surrounding small islands have some highly active volcanoes. This is due to Sicily being geographically on the northern edge of the [African Plate](/wiki/African_Plate "African Plate").[[88]](#cite_note-89) Mount Etna is the largest active volcano in Europe and casts black ash over the island with its recurrent eruptions. It stands 3,403 metres (11,165 ft) high as of September 2024.[[89]](#cite_note-90) It is the highest mountain in Italy south of the [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps"). Etna covers an area of 1,190 km2 (459 sq mi) with a basal circumference of 140 km (87 mi). This makes it the largest of the three [active volcanoes in Italy](/wiki/Volcanism_in_Italy "Volcanism in Italy"), being about two and a half times the height of the next largest, [Mount Vesuvius](/wiki/Mount_Vesuvius "Mount Vesuvius"). In [Greek mythology](/wiki/Greek_mythology "Greek mythology"), the deadly monster [Typhon](/wiki/Typhon "Typhon") was trapped under the mountain by [Zeus](/wiki/Zeus "Zeus"), the god of the sky.[[90]](#cite_note-91) Mount Etna is widely regarded as a cultural symbol and icon of Sicily.

The [Aeolian Islands](/wiki/Aeolian_Islands "Aeolian Islands") in the [Tyrrhenian Sea](/wiki/Tyrrhenian_Sea "Tyrrhenian Sea"), to the northeast of mainland Sicily form a volcanic complex. The three volcanoes of [Vulcano](/wiki/Vulcano "Vulcano"), [Stromboli](/wiki/Stromboli "Stromboli") and [Lipari](/wiki/Lipari "Lipari") are also active, although the last is usually dormant. Off the southern coast of Sicily, the underwater volcano of [Ferdinandea](/wiki/Ferdinandea "Ferdinandea"), which is part of the larger [Empedocles volcano](/wiki/Empedocles_(volcano) "Empedocles (volcano)"), last erupted in 1831. It is located between the coast of [Agrigento](/wiki/Agrigento "Agrigento") and the island of [Pantelleria](/wiki/Pantelleria "Pantelleria") (which itself is a dormant volcano).

The [autonomous region](/wiki/Autonomous_administrative_division "Autonomous administrative division") also includes several neighbouring islands: the [Aegadian Islands](/wiki/Aegadian_Islands "Aegadian Islands"), the Aeolian Islands, Pantelleria and [Lampedusa](/wiki/Lampedusa "Lampedusa"). From a geographical perspective, also forming a part of Sicily is the Maltese Archipelago, the islands constitute the [republic of Malta](/wiki/Republic_of_Malta "Republic of Malta").

### Mountains

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/EtnaAvi%C3%B3.JPG/250px-EtnaAvi%C3%B3.JPG)](/wiki/File:EtnaAvi%C3%B3.JPG)

The [Monti Sicani](/wiki/Monti_Sicani "Monti Sicani") in western Sicily

The mountains of Sicily form a significant part of the island's diverse landscape, with [Mount Etna](/wiki/Mount_Etna "Mount Etna"), one of the world's most active volcanoes, being the highest and most notable peak. Other important mountain ranges include the [Nebrodi](/wiki/Nebrodi "Nebrodi"), [Madonie](/wiki/Madonie "Madonie") and [Peloritani](/wiki/Peloritani "Peloritani") mountains ranges.

### Rivers

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Papiro-Ciane.jpg/250px-Papiro-Ciane.jpg)](/wiki/File:Papiro-Ciane.jpg)

View of the [Ciane](/wiki/Ciane "Ciane") river

Several rivers [drain](/wiki/Drainage "Drainage") the island, most of which flow through the central area and enter the sea at the south of the island. The [Salso](/wiki/Salso "Salso") flows through parts of Enna and Caltanissetta before entering the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") at the [port](/wiki/Port "Port") of [Licata](/wiki/Licata "Licata"). To the east, the [Alcantara](/wiki/Alcantara_(river) "Alcantara (river)") flows through the province of [Messina](/wiki/Messina "Messina") and enters the sea at [Giardini Naxos](/wiki/Giardini_Naxos "Giardini Naxos"), and the [Simeto](/wiki/Simeto "Simeto"), which flows into the [Ionian Sea](/wiki/Ionian_Sea "Ionian Sea") south of [Catania](/wiki/Catania "Catania"). Other important rivers on the island are the [Belice](/wiki/Belice "Belice") and [Platani](/wiki/Platani_(river) "Platani (river)") in the southwest.

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Climate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Veduta-Vallone-Caltanissetta.jpg/250px-Veduta-Vallone-Caltanissetta.jpg)](/wiki/File:Veduta-Vallone-Caltanissetta.jpg)

Inner Sicily

Sicily has for the most part a typical [Mediterranean climate](/wiki/Mediterranean_climate "Mediterranean climate") ([Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): *Csa*) with mild and wet winters and hot, dry summers with changeable intermediate seasons. On the coasts, especially in the southwest, the climate is affected by the African currents and summers can be hot.

Snow falls above 900 metres, but it can fall in the hills. The interior mountains, especially [Nebrodi](/wiki/Nebrodi "Nebrodi"), [Madonie](/wiki/Madonie "Madonie"), and [Etna](/wiki/Mount_Etna "Mount Etna"), enjoy a mountain climate, with heavy snowfalls during winter. The summit of Mount Etna is usually snow-capped from October to May.

In the summer, the [sirocco](/wiki/Sirocco "Sirocco") – the wind from the Sahara – can be felt. Rainfall is scarce, and in some provinces a water crisis can occur.

According to the Regional Agency for Waste and Water, on 10 August 1999, the weather station of [Catenanuova](/wiki/Catenanuova "Catenanuova") (EN) recorded an unofficial maximum temperature of 48.5 °C (119 °F).[[93]](#cite_note-94) On 11 August 2021, a new [highest temperature record](/wiki/List_of_weather_records "List of weather records") for [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") with a reading of 48.8 °C (119.8 °F) was set near the city of [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_Sicily "Syracuse, Sicily").[[94]](#cite_note-95) Total precipitation is highly variable, generally increasing with elevation. In general, the southern and southeast coast receives the least rainfall (less than 50 cm (20 in)), and the northern and northeastern highlands the most (over 100 cm (39 in)).

### Flora and fauna

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Flora and fauna")]

See also: [List of endemic plants of Sicily](/wiki/List_of_endemic_plants_of_Sicily "List of endemic plants of Sicily")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Benny_Trapp_Podarcis_waglerianus.jpg/250px-Benny_Trapp_Podarcis_waglerianus.jpg)](/wiki/File:Benny_Trapp_Podarcis_waglerianus.jpg)

The [Sicilian wall lizard](/wiki/Sicilian_wall_lizard "Sicilian wall lizard") endemic to Sicily and the [Aegadian Islands](/wiki/Aegadian_Islands "Aegadian Islands")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Canis_lupus_cristaldii_subsp._nov.png/250px-Canis_lupus_cristaldii_subsp._nov.png)](/wiki/File:Canis_lupus_cristaldii_subsp._nov.png)

Extinct [Sicilian wolf](/wiki/Sicilian_wolf "Sicilian wolf"). The subspecies reportedly went extinct due to human persecution in the 1920s, though there were several possible sightings up to the 1970s.

Sicily is an often-quoted example of man-made [deforestation](/wiki/Deforestation "Deforestation"), which has occurred since Roman times when the island was turned into an agricultural region.[[87]](#cite_note-frommers-88) This gradually dried the climate, leading to a decline in rainfall and the drying of rivers. The central and southwest provinces are practically devoid of forest.[[95]](#cite_note-96) In Northern Sicily, there are three important forests; near Mount Etna, in the [Nebrodi Mountains](/wiki/Nebrodi "Nebrodi") and in the [Bosco della Ficuzza](/w/index.php?title=Bosco_della_Ficuzza&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bosco della Ficuzza (page does not exist)") Natural Reserve near [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo"). The Nebrodi Mountains Regional Park, established on 4 August 1993 and covering 86,000 hectares (210,000 acres), is the largest protected natural area of Sicily; it contains the largest forest in Sicily, the [Caronia](/wiki/Caronia "Caronia"). The [Hundred Horse Chestnut](/wiki/Hundred_Horse_Chestnut "Hundred Horse Chestnut") (*Castagno dei Cento Cavalli*), in [Sant'Alfio](/wiki/Sant%27Alfio "Sant'Alfio"), on the eastern slopes of Mount Etna, is the largest and oldest known [chestnut](/wiki/Chestnut "Chestnut") tree in the world at 2,000–4,000 years old.[[96]](#cite_note-97)

Sicily has a wide variety of fauna. Species include the [European wildcat](/wiki/European_wildcat "European wildcat"), [red fox](/wiki/Red_fox "Red fox"), [least weasel](/wiki/Least_weasel "Least weasel"), [pine marten](/wiki/Pine_marten "Pine marten"), [fallow deer](/wiki/Fallow_deer "Fallow deer"),[[97]](#cite_note-98) [wild boar](/wiki/Wild_boar "Wild boar"), [crested porcupine](/wiki/Crested_porcupine "Crested porcupine"), [European hedgehog](/wiki/European_hedgehog "European hedgehog"), [common toad](/wiki/Common_toad "Common toad"), *[Vipera aspis](/wiki/Vipera_aspis "Vipera aspis")*, [golden eagle](/wiki/Golden_eagle "Golden eagle"), [peregrine falcon](/wiki/Peregrine_falcon "Peregrine falcon"), [Eurasian hoopoe](/wiki/Eurasian_hoopoe "Eurasian hoopoe") and [black-winged stilt](/wiki/Black-winged_stilt "Black-winged stilt").[[98]](#cite_note-99) [Roe deer](/wiki/Roe_deer "Roe deer") were driven to extinction on the island.[[99]](#cite_note-100) The [Sicilian wolf](/wiki/Sicilian_wolf "Sicilian wolf") (*Canis lupus cristaldii*) was an [endemic](/wiki/Endemic "Endemic") wolf [subspecies](/wiki/Subspecies_of_Canis_lupus "Subspecies of Canis lupus") that was driven to extinction in the 20th century. During the [Late Pleistocene](/wiki/Late_Pleistocene "Late Pleistocene"), a species of [dwarf elephant](/wiki/Dwarf_elephant "Dwarf elephant"), [Palaeoloxodon mnaidriensis](/wiki/Palaeoloxodon_mnaidriensis "Palaeoloxodon mnaidriensis") inhabited the island, with its latest records on Sicily dating to around 20,000 years ago.[[100]](#cite_note-101)

The [Riserva naturale dello Zingaro](/wiki/Riserva_naturale_dello_Zingaro "Riserva naturale dello Zingaro") (Zingaro Natural Reserve) is one of the best examples of unspoiled coastal wilderness in Sicily.[[101]](#cite_note-102)

[Marine Life of the Straits of Messina](/wiki/Marine_Life_of_the_Straits_of_Messina "Marine Life of the Straits of Messina") includes varieties of birds and marine life, including larger species such as [greater flamingo](/wiki/Greater_flamingo "Greater flamingo") and [fin whale](/wiki/Fin_whale "Fin whale").

As of 2025, Sicily has a population of 4,787,390, making it the [5th most populated region in Italy](/wiki/List_of_regions_of_Italy#List_of_regions "List of regions of Italy"). 48.8% of the population are male and 51.2% are female, compared to the national average of 49.0% and 51.0% respectively. Minors make up 15.9% of the population, and seniors make up 23.7%, compared to the national average of 14.9% and 24.7% respectively.[[2]](#cite_note-population-2)

In the first century after the [Italian unification](/wiki/Italian_unification "Italian unification"), Sicily had one of the most negative [net migration rates](/wiki/Net_migration_rate "Net migration rate") among the regions of Italy because of the emigration of millions of people to Northern Italy, other European countries, North America, South America and Australia. Like the South of Italy and Sardinia, immigration to the island is very low compared to other regions of Italy because workers tend to head to [Northern Italy](/wiki/Northern_Italy "Northern Italy") instead, due to better employment and industrial opportunities. As in the rest of Italy, the official language is Italian and the primary religion is [Roman Catholicism](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism "Roman Catholicism").[[104]](#cite_note-105)[[105]](#cite_note-106)

### Emigration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Emigration")]

See also: [Sicilian Americans](/wiki/Sicilian_Americans "Sicilian Americans")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Tampa_Ybor_City_Italian_Club_window01.jpg/250px-Tampa_Ybor_City_Italian_Club_window01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tampa_Ybor_City_Italian_Club_window01.jpg)

The Sicilian [triskelion](/wiki/Triskelion "Triskelion") in [Ybor City](/wiki/Ybor_City "Ybor City"), a district of [Tampa, Florida](/wiki/Tampa,_Florida "Tampa, Florida"), home to many [Sicilian Americans](/wiki/Sicilian_Americans "Sicilian Americans")

Sicilian emigration started shortly after [Italian unification](/wiki/Italian_unification "Italian unification") and has continued into the present day. The aforementioned factors, along with a failed land reform, resulted in an unprecedented wave of Sicilians emigrating, [first to the United States](/wiki/Sicilian_Americans "Sicilian Americans") between the 1880s and the 1920s and later to northern Italy; since the 1960s, other population destinations include [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), [France](/wiki/France "France"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland"), as well as [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") and [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America"). An estimated 10 million people of Sicilian origin live around the world.

The trend of emigration, particularly among young Sicilians leaving the island in search of employment elsewhere in Italy and abroad, continues in early 21st century.[[106]](#cite_note-107) Sicily remains the Italian region with the highest number of [expatriates](/wiki/Expatriate "Expatriate"): as of 2017, over 750,000 Sicilians, 14.4% of the island's population, lived abroad.[[107]](#cite_note-108)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Religion")]

See also: [Italo-Albanian Catholic Church](/wiki/Italo-Albanian_Catholic_Church "Italo-Albanian Catholic Church"), [History of the Jews in Sicily](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Sicily "History of the Jews in Sicily"), and [History of Islam in southern Italy](/wiki/History_of_Islam_in_southern_Italy "History of Islam in southern Italy")

As in most Italian regions, [Roman Catholicism](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism "Roman Catholicism") is the predominant religious denomination in Sicily, and the church still plays an important role in the lives of most people. There is also a notable small minority of Eastern-rite [Byzantine Catholics](/wiki/Byzantine_Catholics "Byzantine Catholics") which has a mixed congregation of ethnic [Albanians](/wiki/Albanians "Albanians"); it is operated by the [Italo-Albanian Catholic Church](/wiki/Italo-Albanian_Catholic_Church "Italo-Albanian Catholic Church"). Most people still attend church weekly or at least for religious festivals, and many people get married in churches. There was a wide presence of Jews in Sicily for at least 1,400 years and possibly for more than 2,000 years. Some scholars believe that the Sicilian Jewry are partial ancestors of the [Ashkenazi Jews](/wiki/Ashkenazi_Jews "Ashkenazi Jews").[[109]](#cite_note-110) However, much of the Jewish community faded away when they were [expelled from the island](/wiki/Expulsion_of_the_Jews_from_Sicily "Expulsion of the Jews from Sicily") in 1492. [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") was present during the [Emirate of Sicily](/wiki/Emirate_of_Sicily "Emirate of Sicily"), although Muslims were also expelled. Today, mostly due to immigration to the island, there are also several religious minorities, such as Jehovah's Witnesses, [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), Islam, Judaism, and [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism"). There are also a some [Evangelical Christians](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism") who live on the island. As of 2020, there are approximately 4,989,921 Roman Catholics in Sicily constituting about 99.2% of the island's population. Additionally there are also about 23,120 members of the [Italo-Albanian Catholic Church](/wiki/Italo-Albanian_Catholic_Church "Italo-Albanian Catholic Church") also living in Sicily constituting roughly 0.5% of the population.[[110]](#cite_note-111)

## Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Politics")]

Main article: [Politics of Sicily](/wiki/Politics_of_Sicily "Politics of Sicily")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Normandiarren_gaztelua.jpg/250px-Normandiarren_gaztelua.jpg)](/wiki/File:Normandiarren_gaztelua.jpg)

[Palazzo dei Normanni](/wiki/Palazzo_dei_Normanni "Palazzo dei Normanni"), the seat of the [Sicilian Regional Assembly](/wiki/Sicilian_Regional_Assembly "Sicilian Regional Assembly"). It was a 9th-century Arab palace, converted by the [Normans](/wiki/Norman_conquest_of_southern_Italy "Norman conquest of southern Italy") into their governing castle.

The politics of Sicily takes place in a framework of a [presidential](/wiki/Presidential_system "Presidential system") [representative democracy](/wiki/Representative_democracy "Representative democracy"), whereby the President of Regional Government is the [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government"), and of a pluriform [multi-party system](/wiki/Multi-party_system "Multi-party system"). [Executive power](/wiki/Executive_power "Executive power") is exercised by the Regional Government. [Legislative power](/wiki/Legislative_power "Legislative power") is vested in both the government and the [Sicilian Regional Assembly](/wiki/Sicilian_Regional_Assembly "Sicilian Regional Assembly"). The capital of Sicily is [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo").

Traditionally, Sicily votes for centre-right parties during elections.[[111]](#cite_note-112) From 1943 to 1951, there was also a [separatist](/wiki/Separatism "Separatism") political party called [Sicilian Independence Movement](/wiki/Sicilian_Independence_Movement "Sicilian Independence Movement") (*Movimento Indipendentista Siciliano*, MIS). Their most successful result was at the [1946 general election](/wiki/Italian_general_election,_1946 "Italian general election, 1946"), when MIS obtained 0.7% of national votes (8.8% of votes in Sicily), and four seats. However, the movement lost all their seats following the [1948 general election](/wiki/Italian_general_election,_1948 "Italian general election, 1948") and the 1951 regional election. Even though it has never been formally disbanded, today the movement is no longer part of the [politics of Sicily](/wiki/Politics_of_Sicily "Politics of Sicily"). After [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), Sicily became a stronghold of the [Christian Democracy](/wiki/Christian_Democracy_(Italy) "Christian Democracy (Italy)"). Sicily is now governed by a centre-right coalition. [Renato Schifani](/wiki/Renato_Schifani "Renato Schifani") is the current President and has served since 2022.

### Administrative divisions

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Provinces_of_Sicily_map.png/250px-Provinces_of_Sicily_map.png)](/wiki/File:Provinces_of_Sicily_map.png)

Provinces of Sicily

Administratively, Sicily is divided into 3 [metropolitan cities](/wiki/Metropolitan_cities_of_Italy "Metropolitan cities of Italy") and 6 *free municipal consortiums* (*libero consorzio comunale*), each with a capital city of the same name as the province. Small surrounding islands are also part of various Sicilian provinces: the [Aeolian Islands](/wiki/Aeolian_Islands "Aeolian Islands") (Messina), isle of [Ustica](/wiki/Ustica "Ustica") (Palermo), [Aegadian Islands](/wiki/Aegadian_Islands "Aegadian Islands") (Trapani), isle of [Pantelleria](/wiki/Pantelleria "Pantelleria") (Trapani) and [Pelagian Islands](/wiki/Pelagian_Islands "Pelagian Islands") (Agrigento).
