## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Economy")]

See also: [Economy of Italy](/wiki/Economy_of_Italy "Economy of Italy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Olives_%289520845387%29.jpg/250px-Olives_%289520845387%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Olives_(9520845387).jpg)

Olive groves

Thanks to regular growth, Sicily is the eighth largest regional economy of Italy in terms of total GDP (see [List of Italian regions by GDP](/wiki/List_of_Italian_regions_by_GDP_(PPP) "List of Italian regions by GDP (PPP)")). A series of reforms and investments in agriculture, such as the introduction of modern irrigation systems, has made this important industry competitive.[[112]](#cite_note-113) In the 1970s, some factories were opened, resulting in growth in the [industrial sector](/wiki/Industrial_sector "Industrial sector").[[113]](#cite_note-114) In recent years the [service industry](/wiki/Service_industry "Service industry") has become more important due to the opening of several shopping malls and some modest growth in financial and telecommunication activities.[[114]](#cite_note-115) Tourism is an important source of income for the island, which attracts visitors due to its rich natural and historical heritage. Today Sicily is investing a large amount of money into the development of its [hospitality industry](/wiki/Hospitality_industry "Hospitality industry"), to attract even more tourism.[[115]](#cite_note-116) However, Sicily continues to have a GDP per capita below the Italian average, and higher unemployment than the rest of Italy.[[116]](#cite_note-117)

### Agriculture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Agriculture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Marsala_Wine.jpg/250px-Marsala_Wine.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marsala_Wine.jpg)

A sample of [Marsala](/wiki/Marsala_wine "Marsala wine"), a [DOC](/wiki/Denominazione_di_origine_controllata "Denominazione di origine controllata") wine produced in the city of [Marsala](/wiki/Marsala "Marsala")

Sicily has long been noted for its fertile soil, which is the result of past volcanic eruptions. The local agriculture is also helped by the pleasant climate of the island. The main agricultural products are wheat, [citrons](/wiki/Diamante_citron "Diamante citron"), oranges *([Arancia Rossa di Sicilia IGP](/wiki/Blood_orange "Blood orange"))*, lemons, tomatoes *([Pomodoro di Pachino IGP](/wiki/Pomodoro_di_Pachino "Pomodoro di Pachino"))*, [olives](/wiki/Olive "Olive"), [olive oil](/wiki/Olive_oil "Olive oil"), [artichokes](/wiki/Artichoke "Artichoke"), [prickly pear](/wiki/Opuntia_ficus-indica "Opuntia ficus-indica") *(Fico d'India dell'[Etna](/wiki/Mount_Etna "Mount Etna") DOP)*, [almonds](/wiki/Almond "Almond"), [grapes](/wiki/Grape "Grape"), [pistachios](/wiki/Pistachio "Pistachio") *(Pistacchio di [Bronte](/wiki/Bronte,_Sicily "Bronte, Sicily") DOP)* and wine. Cattle and sheep are raised. The production of cheese is particularly important thanks to the [Ragusano DOP](/wiki/Ragusano_cheese "Ragusano cheese") and the [Pecorino Siciliano DOP](/wiki/Pecorino_Siciliano "Pecorino Siciliano"). [Ragusa](/wiki/Ragusa,_Italy "Ragusa, Italy") is noted for its [honey](/wiki/Honey "Honey") (*Miele Ibleo*) and chocolate (*[Cioccolato di Modica](/wiki/Cioccolato_di_Modica "Cioccolato di Modica") IGP*) products.[[117]](#cite_note-118)[[118]](#cite_note-119)[[119]](#cite_note-120)[[120]](#cite_note-insicilia-121)[[121]](#cite_note-122)

Sicily is the third largest wine producer in Italy, after [Veneto](/wiki/Veneto "Veneto") and [Emilia Romagna](/wiki/Emilia_Romagna "Emilia Romagna") (and Italy is the world's largest wine producer).[[122]](#cite_note-123) The region is known mainly for fortified [Marsala wines](/wiki/Marsala_wine "Marsala wine"). In recent decades the wine industry has improved, new winemakers are experimenting with less-well-known native varieties, and Sicilian wines have become better known.[[123]](#cite_note-124) The best known local variety is [Nero d'Avola](/wiki/Nero_d%27Avola "Nero d'Avola") named after [Avola](/wiki/Avola "Avola"), a town not far from [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_Sicily "Syracuse, Sicily"). Other important native varieties are: [Nerello Mascalese](/wiki/Nerello "Nerello"), used to make the [Etna Rosso DOC wine](/wiki/Etna_DOC "Etna DOC"); [Frappato](/wiki/Frappato "Frappato"), a component of the [Cerasuolo di Vittoria DOCG wine](/wiki/Frappato "Frappato"); [Moscato di Pantelleria](/wiki/Muscat_of_Alexandria "Muscat of Alexandria") (also known as *Zibibbo*), which is used to make different [Pantelleria](/wiki/Pantelleria "Pantelleria") wines; [Malvasia di Lipari](/wiki/Malvasia#Italian_varieties "Malvasia"), used for the [Malvasia di Lipari DOC wine](/wiki/Malvasia#Italian_varieties "Malvasia"); and [Catarratto](/wiki/Catarratto "Catarratto"), mostly used to make a white wine, [Alcamo DOC](/wiki/Alcamo_wine "Alcamo wine"). Furthermore, in Sicily, high-quality wines are also produced using non-native varieties like [Syrah](/wiki/Syrah "Syrah"), [Chardonnay](/wiki/Chardonnay "Chardonnay") and [Merlot](/wiki/Merlot "Merlot").[[124]](#cite_note-125)

Sicily is also known for its liqueurs, such as [Amaro Averna](/wiki/Amaro_Averna "Amaro Averna"), produced in [Caltanissetta](/wiki/Caltanissetta "Caltanissetta"), and the local [limoncello](/wiki/Limoncello "Limoncello").

Fishing is another fundamental resource for Sicily. It has important [tuna](/wiki/Tuna "Tuna"), [sardine](/wiki/Sardine "Sardine"), [swordfish](/wiki/Swordfish "Swordfish") and [European anchovy](/wiki/European_anchovy "European anchovy") fisheries. [Mazara del Vallo](/wiki/Mazara_del_Vallo "Mazara del Vallo") is the largest fishing centre in Sicily and one of the most important in Italy.[[125]](#cite_note-esploriamo.com-126)

### Industry and manufacturing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Industry and manufacturing")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Palermo-Sicilia-Italy_-_Creative_Commons_by_gnuckx_%283628423942%29.jpg/250px-Palermo-Sicilia-Italy_-_Creative_Commons_by_gnuckx_%283628423942%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palermo-Sicilia-Italy_-_Creative_Commons_by_gnuckx_(3628423942).jpg)

[Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo") shipyards

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/TrivelleRagusaS1.jpg/250px-TrivelleRagusaS1.jpg)](/wiki/File:TrivelleRagusaS1.jpg)

Oilfields near [Ragusa](/wiki/Ragusa,_Italy "Ragusa, Italy")

Improvements in Sicily's road system have helped to promote industrial development. The region has three important [industrial districts](/wiki/Industrial_district "Industrial district"):

* *[Catania](/wiki/Catania "Catania") Industrial District*, where there are several [food industries](/wiki/Food_industries "Food industries") and one of the best European [electronics industry](/wiki/Electronics_industry "Electronics industry") centres called *Etna Valley* (in honour of the best known [Silicon Valley](/wiki/Silicon_Valley "Silicon Valley").) It contains offices and factories of international companies such as [STMicroelectronics](/wiki/STMicroelectronics "STMicroelectronics") and [Numonyx](/wiki/Numonyx "Numonyx");[[125]](#cite_note-esploriamo.com-126)[[126]](#cite_note-127)
* *[Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_Sicily "Syracuse, Sicily") Petrochemical District* with [chemical industries](/wiki/Chemical_industry "Chemical industry"), [oil refineries](/wiki/Oil_refineries "Oil refineries") and important [power stations](/wiki/Power_stations "Power stations") (as the innovative [Archimede combined cycle power plant](/wiki/Archimede_combined_cycle_power_plant "Archimede combined cycle power plant"));[[127]](#cite_note-128)
* the latest *[Enna](/wiki/Enna "Enna") Industrial District* in which there are [food industries](/wiki/Food_industries "Food industries").[[128]](#cite_note-129)

In [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo") there are important [shipyards](/wiki/Shipyards "Shipyards") (such as [Fincantieri](/wiki/Fincantieri "Fincantieri")), [mechanical](/wiki/Mechanical_engineering "Mechanical engineering") factories of famous Italian companies as [Ansaldo Breda](/wiki/Ansaldo_Breda "Ansaldo Breda"), publishing and textile industries. [Chemical industries](/wiki/Chemical_industry "Chemical industry") are also in the [Province of Messina](/wiki/Province_of_Messina "Province of Messina") ([Milazzo](/wiki/Milazzo "Milazzo")) and in the [Province of Caltanissetta](/wiki/Province_of_Caltanissetta "Province of Caltanissetta") ([Gela](/wiki/Gela "Gela")).[[120]](#cite_note-insicilia-121)
There are petroleum, natural gas and [asphalt](/wiki/Bitumen "Bitumen") fields in the Southeast (mostly near [Ragusa](/wiki/Ragusa,_Italy "Ragusa, Italy")) and massive deposits of [halite](/wiki/Halite "Halite") in Central Sicily.[[129]](#cite_note-130) The [Province of Trapani](/wiki/Province_of_Trapani "Province of Trapani") is one of the largest [sea salt](/wiki/Sea_salt "Sea salt") producers in Italy.[[130]](#cite_note-131)

### Roads

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Autostrada_A20_Torregrotta.jpg/250px-Autostrada_A20_Torregrotta.jpg)](/wiki/File:Autostrada_A20_Torregrotta.jpg)

[Messina tramway system](/wiki/Trams_in_Messina "Trams in Messina")

Highways have been built and expanded in the last four decades. The most prominent Sicilian roads are the motorways (known as *autostrade*) in the north of the island. Much of the motorway network is elevated on pillars due to the island's mountainous terrain.[[135]](#cite_note-136)[[136]](#cite_note-137)[[137]](#cite_note-138)[[138]](#cite_note-139) Other main roads in Sicily are the *Strade Statali*, such as the SS.113 that connects [Trapani](/wiki/Trapani "Trapani") to Messina (via Palermo), the SS.114 Messina-[Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_Sicily "Syracuse, Sicily") (via Catania) and the SS.115 Syracuse-Trapani (via [Ragusa](/wiki/Ragusa,_Italy "Ragusa, Italy"), [Gela](/wiki/Gela "Gela") and [Agrigento](/wiki/Agrigento "Agrigento")).

### Railways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Railways")]

Main article: [Railway network of Sicily](/wiki/Railway_network_of_Sicily "Railway network of Sicily")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/%22Pra%C3%A7a_da_Esta%C3%A7%C3%A3o%22_de_Palermo_%281036620145%29.jpg/250px-%22Pra%C3%A7a_da_Esta%C3%A7%C3%A3o%22_de_Palermo_%281036620145%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:%22Pra%C3%A7a_da_Esta%C3%A7%C3%A3o%22_de_Palermo_(1036620145).jpg)

[Palermo Centrale railway station](/wiki/Palermo_Centrale_railway_station "Palermo Centrale railway station")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Punta_Raisi_staz_ferr_treni.jpg/250px-Punta_Raisi_staz_ferr_treni.jpg)](/wiki/File:Punta_Raisi_staz_ferr_treni.jpg)

Two trains inside [Punta Raisi railway station](/wiki/Punta_Raisi_railway_station "Punta Raisi railway station") within [Palermo International Airport](/wiki/Palermo_International_Airport "Palermo International Airport")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/L%27elettrotreno_M.88-08_alla_stazione_di_Giovanni_XXIII.jpg/250px-L%27elettrotreno_M.88-08_alla_stazione_di_Giovanni_XXIII.jpg)](/wiki/File:L%27elettrotreno_M.88-08_alla_stazione_di_Giovanni_XXIII.jpg)

[Catania Metro](/wiki/Catania_Metro "Catania Metro")

The first railway in Sicily was opened in 1863 (Palermo-Bagheria) and today all of the Sicilian provinces are served by a network of railway services, linking to most major cities and towns; this service is operated by [Trenitalia](/wiki/Trenitalia "Trenitalia"). Of the 1,378 km (856 mi) of railway tracks in use, over 60% has been [electrified](/wiki/Railway_electrification_system "Railway electrification system") whilst the remaining 583 km (362 mi) are serviced by [diesel](/wiki/Dieselisation "Dieselisation") engines. 88% of the lines (1.209 km) are single-track and only 169 km (105 mi) are double-track serving the two main routes, Messina-Palermo ([Tyrrhenian](/wiki/Tyrrhenian_Sea "Tyrrhenian Sea")) and Messina-Catania-Syracuse ([Ionian](/wiki/Ionian_Sea "Ionian Sea")), which are the main lines of this region. Of the [narrow-gauge railways](/wiki/Narrow-gauge_railway "Narrow-gauge railway") the [Ferrovia Circumetnea](/wiki/Ferrovia_Circumetnea "Ferrovia Circumetnea") is the only one that still operates, going round [Mount Etna](/wiki/Mount_Etna "Mount Etna"). From the major cities of Sicily, there are services to [Naples](/wiki/Naples "Naples"), [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") and [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan"); this is achieved by the trains being loaded onto [ferries](/wiki/Ferries "Ferries") which cross the Strait.[[139]](#cite_note-140)

In [Catania](/wiki/Catania "Catania") there is an [underground railway](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") service ([metropolitana di Catania](/wiki/Metropolitana_di_Catania "Metropolitana di Catania")); in [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo") the national railway operator [Trenitalia](/wiki/Trenitalia "Trenitalia") operates a [commuter rail](/wiki/Commuter_rail "Commuter rail") ([Palermo metropolitan railway service](/wiki/Palermo_metropolitan_railway_service "Palermo metropolitan railway service")), the Sicilian Capital is also served by 4 AMAT (Comunal Public Transport Operator) tramlines; [Messina](/wiki/Messina "Messina") is served by a [tramline](/wiki/Trams_in_Messina "Trams in Messina").

### Airports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Airports")]

Main article: [List of airports in Sicily](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_Sicily "List of airports in Sicily")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Aeroporto_di_Catania_Terminal_Check-in.jpeg/250px-Aeroporto_di_Catania_Terminal_Check-in.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Aeroporto_di_Catania_Terminal_Check-in.jpeg)

[Catania International Airport](/wiki/Catania%E2%80%93Fontanarossa_Airport "Catania–Fontanarossa Airport")

Sicily has several airports that serve numerous Italian and European destinations and some extra-European.

* [Catania-Fontanarossa Airport](/wiki/Catania-Fontanarossa_Airport "Catania-Fontanarossa Airport"), located on the east coast, is the busiest on the island (and one of the busiest in all of Italy).
* [Palermo International Airport](/wiki/Palermo_International_Airport "Palermo International Airport"), which is also a substantially large airport with many national and international flights.
* [Trapani-Birgi Airport](/wiki/Trapani-Birgi_Airport "Trapani-Birgi Airport"), a military-civil joint-use airport (third for traffic on the island). Recently the airport has seen an increase in traffic thanks to the low-cost carrier [Ryanair](/wiki/Ryanair "Ryanair").
* [Comiso-Ragusa Airport](/wiki/Comiso_Airport "Comiso Airport"), has recently been refurbished and re-converted from military use to a civil airport. It was opened to commercial traffic and general aviation on 30 May 2013.
* [Palermo-Boccadifalco Airport](/wiki/Palermo-Boccadifalco_Airport "Palermo-Boccadifalco Airport") is the old airport of Palermo and is currently used for [general aviation](/wiki/General_aviation "General aviation") and as a base for the [Guardia di Finanza](/wiki/Guardia_di_Finanza "Guardia di Finanza") and [police helicopters](/wiki/Police_helicopter "Police helicopter").
* [NAS Sigonella Airport](/wiki/Naval_Air_Station_Sigonella "Naval Air Station Sigonella"), it is an Italian Air Force and US Navy installation.
* [Lampedusa Airport](/wiki/Lampedusa_Airport "Lampedusa Airport").
* [Pantelleria Airport](/wiki/Pantelleria_Airport "Pantelleria Airport").

### Ports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Ports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Messina_harbour_AK.jpg/250px-Messina_harbour_AK.jpg)](/wiki/File:Messina_harbour_AK.jpg)

[Port of Messina](/wiki/Port_of_Messina "Port of Messina")

By sea, Sicily is served by several ferry routes and cargo ports, and in all major cities, cruise ships dock on a regular basis.

* Mainland Italy: Ports connecting to the mainland are [Messina](/wiki/Messina "Messina") (route to [Villa San Giovanni](/wiki/Villa_San_Giovanni "Villa San Giovanni") and [Salerno](/wiki/Salerno "Salerno")), the busiest passenger port in Italy, [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo") (routes to [Genoa](/wiki/Genoa "Genoa"), [Civitavecchia](/wiki/Civitavecchia "Civitavecchia") and [Naples](/wiki/Naples "Naples")) and [Catania](/wiki/Catania "Catania") (route to [Naples](/wiki/Naples "Naples")).
* Sicily's small surrounding islands: The port of [Milazzo](/wiki/Milazzo "Milazzo") serves the [Aeolian Islands](/wiki/Aeolian_Islands "Aeolian Islands"), the ports of [Trapani](/wiki/Trapani "Trapani") and [Marsala](/wiki/Marsala "Marsala") the [Aegadian Islands](/wiki/Aegadian_Islands "Aegadian Islands") and the port of [Porto Empedocle](/wiki/Porto_Empedocle "Porto Empedocle") the [Pelagie Islands](/wiki/Pelagie_Islands "Pelagie Islands"). From Palermo there is a service to the island of [Ustica](/wiki/Ustica "Ustica") and to [Sardinia](/wiki/Sardinia "Sardinia").
* International connections: From Palermo and Trapani there are weekly services to [Tunisia](/wiki/Tunisia "Tunisia") and there is also a daily service between [Malta](/wiki/Malta "Malta") and [Pozzallo](/wiki/Port_of_Pozzallo "Port of Pozzallo").[[140]](#cite_note-141)[[141]](#cite_note-142)
* Commercial and cargo ports: The port of [Augusta](/wiki/Augusta,_Sicily "Augusta, Sicily") is the fifth-largest cargo port in Italy and handles tonnes of goods. Other major cargo ports are Palermo, Catania, Trapani, [Pozzallo](/wiki/Port_of_Pozzallo "Port of Pozzallo") and [Termini Imerese](/wiki/Termini_Imerese "Termini Imerese").
* Touristic ports: Several ports along the Sicilian coast are in the service of private boats that need to moor on the island. The main ports for this traffic are in [Marina di Ragusa](/wiki/Marina_di_Ragusa "Marina di Ragusa"), [Riposto](/wiki/Riposto "Riposto"), [Portorosa](/w/index.php?title=Portorosa&action=edit&redlink=1 "Portorosa (page does not exist)"), [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_Sicily "Syracuse, Sicily"), [Cefalù](/wiki/Cefal%C3%B9 "Cefalù") and [Sciacca](/wiki/Sciacca "Sciacca"). In Sicily, Palermo is also a major centre for boat rental, with or without crew, in the Mediterranean.
* Fishing ports: Like all islands, Sicily also has many fishing ports. The most important is in [Mazara del Vallo](/wiki/Mazara_del_Vallo "Mazara del Vallo") followed by [Castellamare del Golfo](/wiki/Castellamare_del_Golfo "Castellamare del Golfo"), [Licata](/wiki/Licata "Licata"), [Scoglitti](/wiki/Scoglitti "Scoglitti") and [Portopalo di Capo Passero](/wiki/Portopalo_di_Capo_Passero "Portopalo di Capo Passero").

### Strait of Messina Bridge

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Strait of Messina Bridge")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Ponte_di_messina.jpg/500px-Ponte_di_messina.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ponte_di_messina.jpg)

Cross-sectional diagram of the [Strait of Messina Bridge](/wiki/Strait_of_Messina_Bridge "Strait of Messina Bridge")

The [Strait of Messina Bridge](/wiki/Strait_of_Messina_Bridge "Strait of Messina Bridge") is a proposed 3.6 km (2.2 mi) [suspension bridge](/wiki/Suspension_bridge "Suspension bridge") across the [Strait of Messina](/wiki/Strait_of_Messina "Strait of Messina"), connecting [Torre Faro](/wiki/Torre_Faro "Torre Faro") in Sicily with [Villa San Giovanni](/wiki/Villa_San_Giovanni "Villa San Giovanni") on the Italian peninsula.[[142]](#cite_note-143)

While a bridge across the Strait of Messina had been proposed since ancient times, the first detailed plan was made in the 1990s for a suspension bridge. The project was cancelled in 2006 under prime minister [Romano Prodi](/wiki/Romano_Prodi "Romano Prodi").[[143]](#cite_note-plans-dropped-bbc-144) On 6 March 2009, as part of a massive new public works programme, prime minister [Silvio Berlusconi](/wiki/Silvio_Berlusconi "Silvio Berlusconi")'s government announced that construction of the Messina Bridge would indeed go ahead, pledging €1.3 billion as a contribution to the total cost, estimated at €6.1 billion.[[144]](#cite_note-145) The project was cancelled again on 26 February 2013, by prime minister [Mario Monti](/wiki/Mario_Monti "Mario Monti")'s government, due to budget constraints.[[145]](#cite_note-sinks-146) A decade later, the project was revived again with a decree by [Giorgia Meloni](/wiki/Giorgia_Meloni "Giorgia Meloni")'s government, on 16 March 2023,[[146]](#cite_note-approves-147) which received presidential approval on 31 March 2023.[[147]](#cite_note-Stretto-148)[[148]](#cite_note-149)

The bridge was fully approved by the Meloni government in August 2025, with construction set to begin in the autumn of 2025.[[149]](#cite_note-150) It will be the [longest suspension bridge in the world](/wiki/List_of_largest_suspension_bridges "List of largest suspension bridges") when it opens in 2032. The bridge would also be part of the [Berlin–Palermo railway axis](/wiki/Berlin%E2%80%93Palermo_railway_axis "Berlin–Palermo railway axis") (Line 1) of the [Trans-European Transport Networks](/wiki/Trans-European_Transport_Networks "Trans-European Transport Networks") (TEN-T).[[150]](#cite_note-ansa-151)

## Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Tourism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Spiaggia_Isola_dei_Coniglio_Lampedusa.JPG/250px-Spiaggia_Isola_dei_Coniglio_Lampedusa.JPG)](/wiki/File:Spiaggia_Isola_dei_Coniglio_Lampedusa.JPG)

[Lampedusa](/wiki/Lampedusa "Lampedusa"), [Pelagie Islands](/wiki/Pelagie_Islands "Pelagie Islands")

Sicily's sunny, dry climate, scenery, cuisine, history, and architecture attract many tourists from the rest of Italy and abroad. The tourist season peaks in the summer months, although people visit the island all year round. [Mount Etna](/wiki/Mount_Etna "Mount Etna"), the beaches, the archaeological sites, and major cities such as [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo"), [Catania](/wiki/Catania "Catania"), [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_Sicily "Syracuse, Sicily") and [Ragusa](/wiki/Ragusa,_Sicily "Ragusa, Sicily") are the favourite tourist destinations, but the old town of [Taormina](/wiki/Taormina "Taormina") and the neighbouring seaside resort of [Giardini Naxos](/wiki/Giardini_Naxos "Giardini Naxos") draw visitors from all over the world, as do the [Aeolian Islands](/wiki/Aeolian_Islands "Aeolian Islands"), [Erice](/wiki/Erice "Erice"), [Terrasini](/wiki/Terrasini "Terrasini"), [Castellammare del Golfo](/wiki/Castellammare_del_Golfo "Castellammare del Golfo"), [Cefalù](/wiki/Cefal%C3%B9 "Cefalù"), [Agrigento](/wiki/Agrigento "Agrigento"), the [Pelagie Islands](/wiki/Pelagie_Islands "Pelagie Islands") and [Capo d'Orlando](/wiki/Capo_d%27Orlando "Capo d'Orlando"). The last features some of the best-preserved temples of the ancient Greek period. Many Mediterranean cruise ships stop in Sicily, and many wine tourists also visit the island.

Some scenes of several Hollywood and [Cinecittà](/wiki/Cinecitt%C3%A0 "Cinecittà") films were shot in Sicily. This increased the attraction of Sicily as a tourist destination.[[151]](#cite_note-152)

### UNESCO World Heritage Sites

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: UNESCO World Heritage Sites")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Villa_romana_di_Piazza_Armerina_-_Sicilia_-_tigre.JPG/250px-Villa_romana_di_Piazza_Armerina_-_Sicilia_-_tigre.JPG)](/wiki/File:Villa_romana_di_Piazza_Armerina_-_Sicilia_-_tigre.JPG)

One of the mosaics in [Villa Romana del Casale](/wiki/Villa_Romana_del_Casale "Villa Romana del Casale"), a large and elaborate [Roman villa](/wiki/Roman_villa "Roman villa") or palace located about 3 km from the town of [Piazza Armerina](/wiki/Piazza_Armerina "Piazza Armerina")

There are seven [UNESCO World Heritage Sites](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Sites "UNESCO World Heritage Sites") on Sicily. By the order of inscription:

* **[Valle dei Templi](/wiki/Valle_dei_Templi "Valle dei Templi")** (1997) is one of the most outstanding examples of [Magna Graecia](/wiki/Magna_Graecia "Magna Graecia") art and architecture, and is one of the main attractions of Sicily as well as a national monument of Italy. The site is located in [Agrigento](/wiki/Agrigento "Agrigento").[[152]](#cite_note-153)
* **[Villa Romana del Casale](/wiki/Villa_Romana_del_Casale "Villa Romana del Casale")** (1997) is a [Roman villa](/wiki/Roman_villa "Roman villa") built in the first quarter of the 4th century and located about 3 km (2 mi) outside the town of [Piazza Armerina](/wiki/Piazza_Armerina "Piazza Armerina"). It contains the richest, largest and most complex collection of Roman mosaics in the world.[[153]](#cite_note-154)
* **[Aeolian Islands](/wiki/Aeolian_Islands "Aeolian Islands")** (2000) are a [volcanic](/wiki/Volcano "Volcano") [archipelago](/wiki/Archipelago "Archipelago") in the [Tyrrhenian Sea](/wiki/Tyrrhenian_Sea "Tyrrhenian Sea"), named after the demigod of the winds [Aeolus](/wiki/Aeolus "Aeolus"). The Aeolian Islands are a tourist destination in the summer, and attract up to 200,000 visitors annually.[[154]](#cite_note-155)
* **[Late Baroque Towns of the Val di Noto](/wiki/Val_di_Noto "Val di Noto")** (2002) "represent the culmination and final flowering of [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque "Baroque") art in Europe".[[155]](#cite_note-156) It includes several towns: [Caltagirone](/wiki/Caltagirone "Caltagirone"), [Militello in Val di Catania](/wiki/Militello_in_Val_di_Catania "Militello in Val di Catania"), [Catania](/wiki/Catania "Catania"), [Modica](/wiki/Modica "Modica"), [Noto](/wiki/Noto "Noto"), [Palazzolo Acreide](/wiki/Palazzolo_Acreide "Palazzolo Acreide"), [Ragusa](/wiki/Ragusa,_Italy "Ragusa, Italy") and [Scicli](/wiki/Scicli "Scicli").
* **[Necropolis of Pantalica](/wiki/Necropolis_of_Pantalica "Necropolis of Pantalica")** (2005) is a large [Necropolis](/wiki/Necropolis "Necropolis") in Sicily with over 5,000 tombs dating from the 13th to the 7th centuries BC. [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_Sicily "Syracuse, Sicily") is notable for its rich Greek history, culture, amphitheatres and architecture. They are situated in south-eastern Sicily.
* **[Mount Etna](/wiki/Mount_Etna "Mount Etna")** (2013) is one of the most active volcanoes in the world and is in an almost constant state of activity and generated myths, legends and naturalistic observation from Greek, Celts and Roman classic and medieval times.[[156]](#cite_note-157)
* **Arab-Norman [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo") and the cathedral churches of [Cefalù](/wiki/Cefal%C3%B9 "Cefalù") and [Monreale](/wiki/Monreale "Monreale")**; includes a series of nine civil and religious structures dating from the era of the Norman kingdom of Sicily (1130–1194)[[157]](#cite_note-158)

### Archaeological sites

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Archaeological sites")]

Because many different cultures settled, dominated or invaded the island, Sicily has a huge variety of [archaeological sites](/wiki/Archaeological_sites "Archaeological sites"). Also, some of the most notable and best preserved temples and other structures of the Greek world are located in Sicily.[[162]](#cite_note-163) Here is a short list of the major archaeological sites:

* Sicels/Sicans/Elymians/Greeks: [Segesta](/wiki/Segesta "Segesta"), [Eryx](/wiki/Eryx_(Sicily) "Eryx (Sicily)"), [Cava Ispica](/wiki/Ispica "Ispica"), [Thapsos](/wiki/Thapsos "Thapsos"), [Pantalica](/wiki/Pantalica "Pantalica");
* Greeks: [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_Sicily "Syracuse, Sicily"), [Agrigento](/wiki/Agrigento "Agrigento"), [Segesta](/wiki/Segesta "Segesta"), [Selinunte](/wiki/Selinunte "Selinunte"), [Gela](/wiki/Gela "Gela"), [Kamarina](/wiki/Kamarina,_Sicily "Kamarina, Sicily"), [Himera](/wiki/Himera "Himera"), [Megara Hyblaea](/wiki/Megara_Hyblaea "Megara Hyblaea"), [Naxos](/wiki/Naxos_(Sicily) "Naxos (Sicily)"), [Heraclea Minoa](/wiki/Heraclea_Minoa "Heraclea Minoa");
* Phoenicians: [Motya](/wiki/Motya "Motya"), [Soluntum](/wiki/Soluntum "Soluntum"), [Marsala](/wiki/Marsala "Marsala"), [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo");
* Romans: [Piazza Armerina](/wiki/Piazza_Armerina "Piazza Armerina"), [Centuripe](/wiki/Centuripe "Centuripe"), [Taormina](/wiki/Taormina "Taormina"), [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo");

The excavation and restoration of one of Sicily's best known archaeological sites, the [Valley of the Temples](/wiki/Valle_dei_Templi "Valle dei Templi") in Agrigento, was at the direction of the archaeologist [Domenico Antonio Lo Faso Pietrasanta](/wiki/Domenico_Lo_Faso_Pietrasanta "Domenico Lo Faso Pietrasanta"), Fifth Duke of [Serradifalco](/wiki/Serradifalco "Serradifalco"), known in archaeological circles simply as *"Serradifalco"*. He also oversaw the restoration of ancient sites at [Segesta](/wiki/Segesta "Segesta"), [Selinunte](/wiki/Selinunte "Selinunte"), [Siracusa](/wiki/Syracuse,_Sicily "Syracuse, Sicily") and [Taormina](/wiki/Taormina "Taormina").

### Coastal towers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Coastal towers")]

The coastal towers in Sicily (*Torri costiere della Sicilia*) are 218 old [watchtowers](/wiki/Watchtowers "Watchtowers") along the coast. In Sicily, the first coastal towers date back to the late Norman period. From 1360 the threat came from the south, from [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa") to [Maghreb](/wiki/Maghreb "Maghreb"), mainly to [Barbary pirates](/wiki/Barbary_pirates "Barbary pirates") and corsairs of [Barbary Coast](/wiki/Barbary_Coast "Barbary Coast"). In 1516, the Turks settled in [Algiers](/wiki/Algiers "Algiers"), and from 1520, the corsair [Hayreddin Barbarossa](/wiki/Hayreddin_Barbarossa "Hayreddin Barbarossa") under the command of [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") operated from that harbour.

Most existing towers were built on architectural designs of the Florentine architect [Camillo Camilliani](/wiki/Camillo_Camilliani "Camillo Camilliani") from 1583 to 1584 and involved the coastal periple of Sicily. The typology changed completely in '800, because of the new higher fire volumes of cannon vessels, the towers were built on the type of [Martello towers](/wiki/Martello_towers "Martello towers") that the British built in the UK and elsewhere in the British Empire. The decline of Mediterranean piracy caused by the [Second Barbary War](/wiki/Second_Barbary_War "Second Barbary War") led to a smaller number of coastal towers built during the 19th century.[[163]](#cite_note-164)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Culture")]

See also: [List of museums in Sicily](/wiki/List_of_museums_in_Sicily "List of museums in Sicily")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Antonello_da_Messina_-_Virgin_Annunciate_-_Galleria_Regionale_della_Sicilia%2C_Palermo.jpg/250px-Antonello_da_Messina_-_Virgin_Annunciate_-_Galleria_Regionale_della_Sicilia%2C_Palermo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Antonello_da_Messina_-_Virgin_Annunciate_-_Galleria_Regionale_della_Sicilia,_Palermo.jpg)

[*Virgin Annunciate*](/wiki/Virgin_Annunciate_(Antonello_da_Messina,_Palermo) "Virgin Annunciate (Antonello da Messina, Palermo)"), [Antonello da Messina](/wiki/Antonello_da_Messina "Antonello da Messina")

> To have seen Italy without having seen Sicily is to not have seen Italy at all, for Sicily is the clue to everything.

— [Goethe](/wiki/Johann_Wolfgang_von_Goethe "Johann Wolfgang von Goethe")[[168]](#cite_note-169)

Sicily has long been associated with [the arts](/wiki/The_arts "The arts"); many poets, writers, [philosophers](/wiki/Philosophy "Philosophy"), intellectuals, architects and painters have roots on the island. Among the earliest illuminaries there are [Gorgias](/wiki/Gorgias "Gorgias") and [Empedocles](/wiki/Empedocles "Empedocles"), two highly noted Sicilian-Greek philosophers, while the Syracusan-Greek [Epicharmus](/wiki/Epicharmus_of_Kos "Epicharmus of Kos") is held to be the inventor of comedy.[[169]](#cite_note-170)[[170]](#cite_note-171) One of the most famous intellectuals in Greek antiquity was [Archimedes](/wiki/Archimedes "Archimedes"), a [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_Sicily "Syracuse, Sicily") native who is recognized as one of the greatest mathematicians of all time.[[171]](#cite_note-172)

### Art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Art")]

Among the Sicilian artists who succeeded in bringing to the international stage a language of strong civic engagement while at the same time narrating the history of the island, [Mario Bardi](/wiki/Mario_Bardi "Mario Bardi"), a representative of [Existential realism](/w/index.php?title=Existential_realism&action=edit&redlink=1 "Existential realism (page does not exist)"), stands out. His painting, characterized by dramatic tones and a constant attention to [labour](/wiki/Labour_(human_activity) "Labour (human activity)") and [social tensions](/wiki/Southern_Question "Southern Question"), offers a collective and powerful portrayal of Sicily.

### Architecture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Architecture")]

Baglio are traditional living structures in Western Sicily.

#### Ceramics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Ceramics")]

[Terracotta](/wiki/Terracotta "Terracotta") [ceramics](/wiki/Ceramics_(art) "Ceramics (art)") from the island are well known, the art of ceramics on Sicily goes back to the original ancient peoples named the [Sicanians](/wiki/Sicani "Sicani"), it was then perfected during the period of Greek colonisation and is still prominent and distinct to this day.[[172]](#cite_note-173) Nowadays, [Caltagirone](/wiki/Caltagirone "Caltagirone") is one of the most important centres in Sicily for the artistic production of ceramics and terra-cotta sculptures. Famous painters include [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") artist [Antonello da Messina](/wiki/Antonello_da_Messina "Antonello da Messina"), [Pietro Novelli](/wiki/Pietro_Novelli "Pietro Novelli"), [Bruno Caruso](/wiki/Bruno_Caruso "Bruno Caruso"), [Renato Guttuso](/wiki/Renato_Guttuso "Renato Guttuso") and Greek born [Giorgio de Chirico](/wiki/Giorgio_de_Chirico "Giorgio de Chirico") who is commonly dubbed the "father of [Surrealist art](/wiki/Surrealist_art "Surrealist art")" and founder of the [metaphysical art](/wiki/Metaphysical_art "Metaphysical art") movement.[[173]](#cite_note-174) The most noted architects are [Filippo Juvarra](/wiki/Filippo_Juvarra "Filippo Juvarra") (one of the most important figures of the Italian [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque "Baroque")) and [Ernesto Basile](/wiki/Ernesto_Basile "Ernesto Basile").

#### Sicilian Baroque

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Sicilian Baroque")]

Main article: [Sicilian Baroque](/wiki/Sicilian_Baroque "Sicilian Baroque")

The [Sicilian Baroque](/wiki/Sicilian_Baroque "Sicilian Baroque") has a unique architectural identity. [Noto](/wiki/Noto "Noto"), [Caltagirone](/wiki/Caltagirone "Caltagirone"), [Catania](/wiki/Catania "Catania"), [Ragusa](/wiki/Ragusa,_Italy "Ragusa, Italy"), [Modica](/wiki/Modica "Modica"), [Scicli](/wiki/Scicli "Scicli") and particularly [Acireale](/wiki/Acireale "Acireale") contain some of Italy's best examples of [Baroque architecture](/wiki/Baroque_architecture "Baroque architecture"), carved in the local red [sandstone](/wiki/Sandstone "Sandstone"). Noto provides one of the best examples of the Baroque architecture brought to Sicily. The Baroque style in Sicily was largely confined to buildings erected by the church, and [palazzi](/wiki/Palazzo "Palazzo") built as private residences for the Sicilian aristocracy.[[174]](#cite_note-175) The earliest examples of this style in Sicily lacked individuality and were typically heavy-handed pastiches of buildings seen by Sicilian visitors to Rome, [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence"), and [Naples](/wiki/Naples "Naples"). However, even at this early stage, provincial architects had begun to incorporate certain vernacular features of Sicily's older architecture. By the middle of the 18th century, when Sicily's Baroque architecture was noticeably different from that of the mainland, it has a unique freedom of design that is difficult to characterize in words.

### Music and film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Music and film")]

See also: [Music of Sicily](/wiki/Music_of_Sicily "Music of Sicily")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Il_Teatro_Massimo_di_Palermo.jpg/250px-Il_Teatro_Massimo_di_Palermo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Il_Teatro_Massimo_di_Palermo.jpg)

[Teatro Massimo](/wiki/Teatro_Massimo "Teatro Massimo"), [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo")

[Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo") hosts the [Teatro Massimo](/wiki/Teatro_Massimo "Teatro Massimo") which is the largest [opera house](/wiki/Opera_house "Opera house") in Italy and the third largest in all of Europe.[[175]](#cite_note-176) In [Catania](/wiki/Catania "Catania") there is another important [opera house](/wiki/Opera_house "Opera house"), the [Teatro Massimo Bellini](/wiki/Teatro_Massimo_Bellini "Teatro Massimo Bellini") with 1,200 seats, which is considered one of the best European [opera houses](/wiki/Opera_houses "Opera houses") for its acoustics. Sicily's composers vary from [Vincenzo Bellini](/wiki/Vincenzo_Bellini "Vincenzo Bellini"), [Sigismondo d'India](/wiki/Sigismondo_d%27India "Sigismondo d'India"), [Giovanni Pacini](/wiki/Giovanni_Pacini "Giovanni Pacini") and [Alessandro Scarlatti](/wiki/Alessandro_Scarlatti "Alessandro Scarlatti"), to contemporary composers such as [Salvatore Sciarrino](/wiki/Salvatore_Sciarrino "Salvatore Sciarrino") and [Silvio Amato](/wiki/Silvio_Amato "Silvio Amato").

Many films of Italian cinema have been filmed in Sicily, amongst the most noted of which are: [Visconti](/wiki/Luchino_Visconti "Luchino Visconti")'s *"[La Terra Trema](/wiki/La_Terra_Trema "La Terra Trema")"* and *"[Il Gattopardo](/wiki/Il_Gattopardo "Il Gattopardo")"*, [Pietro Germi](/wiki/Pietro_Germi "Pietro Germi")'s *"[Divorzio all'Italiana](/wiki/Divorce,_Italian_Style "Divorce, Italian Style")"* and *"[Sedotta e Abbandonata](/wiki/Seduced_and_Abandoned_(1964_film) "Seduced and Abandoned (1964 film)")"*, [Tornatore](/wiki/Giuseppe_Tornatore "Giuseppe Tornatore")'s *"[Cinema Paradiso](/wiki/Cinema_Paradiso "Cinema Paradiso")"*.

The annual [Taormina Film Fest](/wiki/Taormina_Film_Fest "Taormina Film Fest") takes places in [Taormina](/wiki/Taormina "Taormina").

### Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Literature")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Jacopo_da_Lentini.jpg/250px-Jacopo_da_Lentini.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jacopo_da_Lentini.jpg)

[Giacomo da Lentini](/wiki/Giacomo_da_Lentini "Giacomo da Lentini") (detail of a 14th century [miniature](/wiki/Miniature_(illuminated_manuscript) "Miniature (illuminated manuscript)"), [National Central Library of Florence](/wiki/National_Central_Library_of_Florence "National Central Library of Florence")), a senior poet of the [Sicilian School](/wiki/Sicilian_School "Sicilian School"). Giacomo da Lentini is credited with the invention of the [sonnet](/wiki/Sonnet "Sonnet").[[176]](#cite_note-Giacomo-177)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Luigi_Pirandello_1932.jpg/250px-Luigi_Pirandello_1932.jpg)](/wiki/File:Luigi_Pirandello_1932.jpg)

[Luigi Pirandello](/wiki/Luigi_Pirandello "Luigi Pirandello") ([Nobel laureate, 1934](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature"))

The [Sicilian School](/wiki/Sicilian_School "Sicilian School") was a small community of Sicilian and mainland Italian poets gathered around [Frederick II](/wiki/Frederick_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Frederick II, Holy Roman Emperor"), most of them belonging to his imperial court in [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo"), which was highly influential on [Italian literature](/wiki/Italian_literature "Italian literature"). Headed by [Giacomo da Lentini](/wiki/Giacomo_da_Lentini "Giacomo da Lentini"), they produced more than 300 poems of [courtly love](/wiki/Courtly_love "Courtly love") between 1230 and 1266, the experiment being continued after Frederick's death by his son, [Manfred](/wiki/Manfred_of_Sicily "Manfred of Sicily"). Giacomo da Lentini is credited with the invention of the [sonnet](/wiki/Sonnet "Sonnet").[[176]](#cite_note-Giacomo-177)

These poets drew inspiration from the [troubadour](/wiki/Troubadour "Troubadour") poetry of [Occitania](/wiki/Occitania "Occitania") written in [langue d'oc](/wiki/Langue_d%27oc "Langue d'oc"), which applied the feudal [code of honor](/wiki/Honor_system "Honor system") to the relation between a man (acting as the vassal) and a woman (acting as king or superior). This is a reversal of the traditional role of women, traditionally dependent on men, and marks a new awareness in [medieval](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") society: the decadence of [feudalism](/wiki/Feudalism "Feudalism") with the increasing power of the [middle class](/wiki/Middle_class "Middle class"), causes a shift in the reading public, the [epic](/wiki/Epic_poetry "Epic poetry") (traditionally devoted to great military pursuits) gradually giving way to the [lyric](/wiki/Lyric_poetry "Lyric poetry") (generally focused on love). In the lower Middle Ages more and more women were reading books than ever before and poetry tried to adapt to their point of view and their newly acquired role in society.

This features Occitan poetry, then very influential in Italy. What distinguishes the Sicilian School from the troubadours, however, is the introduction of a kinder, gentler type of woman than that found in their Occitan models; one who was nearer to [Dante](/wiki/Dante_Alighieri "Dante Alighieri")'s madonnas and [Petrarch](/wiki/Petrarch "Petrarch")'s Laura, though much less characterised psychologically. The poems of the Sicilians hardly portray real women or situations (Frederick's song cannot be read as autobiographical), but the style and language are remarkable, since the Sicilians (as Dante called them) created the first literary standard in Italy by enriching the existing vernacular base, probably inspired by popular love songs, with new words of [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language") and [Provençal](/wiki/Occitan_language "Occitan language") origin.

Some of the most noted figures among modern writers and poets are [Luigi Pirandello](/wiki/Luigi_Pirandello "Luigi Pirandello") ([Nobel laureate, 1934](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature")), [Salvatore Quasimodo](/wiki/Salvatore_Quasimodo "Salvatore Quasimodo") ([Nobel laureate, 1959](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature")), [Giovanni Verga](/wiki/Giovanni_Verga "Giovanni Verga") (the father of the *Italian [Verismo](/wiki/Verismo_(literature) "Verismo (literature)")*), [Domenico Tempio](/wiki/Domenico_Tempio "Domenico Tempio"), [Giovanni Meli](/wiki/Giovanni_Meli "Giovanni Meli"), [Luigi Capuana](/wiki/Luigi_Capuana "Luigi Capuana"), [Mario Rapisardi](/wiki/Mario_Rapisardi "Mario Rapisardi"), [Federico de Roberto](/wiki/Federico_de_Roberto "Federico de Roberto"), [Leonardo Sciascia](/wiki/Leonardo_Sciascia "Leonardo Sciascia"), [Vitaliano Brancati](/wiki/Vitaliano_Brancati "Vitaliano Brancati"), [Giuseppe Tomasi di Lampedusa](/wiki/Giuseppe_Tomasi_di_Lampedusa "Giuseppe Tomasi di Lampedusa"), [Elio Vittorini](/wiki/Elio_Vittorini "Elio Vittorini"), [Vincenzo Consolo](/wiki/Vincenzo_Consolo "Vincenzo Consolo") and [Andrea Camilleri](/wiki/Andrea_Camilleri "Andrea Camilleri") (noted for his novels and short stories with the fictional character [Inspector Salvo Montalbano](/wiki/Salvo_Montalbano "Salvo Montalbano") as protagonist). On the political side notable philosophers include [Gaetano Mosca](/wiki/Gaetano_Mosca "Gaetano Mosca") and [Giovanni Gentile](/wiki/Giovanni_Gentile "Giovanni Gentile") who wrote *[The Doctrine of Fascism](/wiki/The_Doctrine_of_Fascism "The Doctrine of Fascism")*.

### Languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Languages")]

Main articles: [Sicilian language](/wiki/Sicilian_language "Sicilian language"), [Regional Italian](/wiki/Regional_Italian "Regional Italian"), [Gallo-Italic of Sicily](/wiki/Gallo-Italic_of_Sicily "Gallo-Italic of Sicily"), and [Arbëresh language](/wiki/Arb%C3%ABresh_language "Arbëresh language")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Dialetti_parlati_in_Sicilia.png/330px-Dialetti_parlati_in_Sicilia.png)](/wiki/File:Dialetti_parlati_in_Sicilia.png)

The languages spoken in Sicily (excluding Italian)

Sicily is mostly [bilingual](/wiki/Multilingualism "Multilingualism"), with most people speaking both Italian and [Sicilian](/wiki/Sicilian_language "Sicilian language"). Sicilian is a [Romance language](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages"), closely related to Italian but distinct from it, with vocabulary influenced by [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"), [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language"), [Norman](/wiki/Norman_language "Norman language"), French, [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"), Spanish and other languages.[[177]](#cite_note-178) Today, however, the use of [Sicilian](/wiki/Sicilian_language "Sicilian language") is limited to informal and family contexts, and is often replaced by a regional variety of Italian (known as *[regional Italian](/wiki/Regional_Italian "Regional Italian") of Sicily*).[[178]](#cite_note-179)[[179]](#cite_note-180) [Varieties](/wiki/Variety_(linguistics) "Variety (linguistics)") related to Sicilian are also spoken in [Calabria](/wiki/Calabria "Calabria") and [Salento](/wiki/Salento "Salento").[[180]](#cite_note-181) Sicilian had a significant influence on the [Maltese language](/wiki/Maltese_language "Maltese language").[[181]](#cite_note-182)

Beyond Sicilian and Italian, other languages are spoken across the island. Within the [province of Palermo](/wiki/Province_of_Palermo "Province of Palermo"), four towns are home to speakers of [Arbëresh](/wiki/Arb%C3%ABresh_language "Arbëresh language"),[[182]](#cite_note-183) which is the name given to varieties of [Albanian](/wiki/Albanian_language "Albanian language") spoken in Italy. The [Arbëreshë people](/wiki/Arb%C3%ABresh%C3%AB_people "Arbëreshë people") originally migrated from the [Balkans](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans") to escape the invasion of the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") in the 15th and 16th centuries and settled in isolated areas of southern Italy.[[183]](#cite_note-184)

In the eastern part of the island, [Gallo-Italic](/wiki/Gallo-Italic_languages "Gallo-Italic languages") varieties, collectively known as [Gallo-Italic of Sicily](/wiki/Gallo-Italic_of_Sicily "Gallo-Italic of Sicily"), are spoken.[[184]](#cite_note-:0-185)[[185]](#cite_note-:1-186) These are related to the other Gallo-Italic languages spoken in most of northern Italy and in other isolated pockets of southern Italy. Gallo-Italic speakers originally migrated to Sicily from southern [Piedmont](/wiki/Piedmont "Piedmont") and [Liguria](/wiki/Liguria "Liguria") to meet the Norman monarchy's need to repopulate strategic centers and control Arab presence in the 11th–13th centuries.[[b]](#cite_note-187)[[184]](#cite_note-:0-185)[[185]](#cite_note-:1-186)

### Science

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Science")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Stanislao_Cannizzaro_01_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Stanislao_Cannizzaro_01_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stanislao_Cannizzaro_01_(cropped).jpg)

[Stanislao Cannizzaro](/wiki/Stanislao_Cannizzaro "Stanislao Cannizzaro"), known for the [Cannizzaro reaction](/wiki/Cannizzaro_reaction "Cannizzaro reaction") and for his influential role in the atomic-weight deliberations of the [Karlsruhe Congress](/wiki/Karlsruhe_Congress "Karlsruhe Congress")

[Catania](/wiki/Catania "Catania") has one of the four laboratories of the [Istituto Nazionale di Fisica Nucleare](/wiki/Istituto_Nazionale_di_Fisica_Nucleare "Istituto Nazionale di Fisica Nucleare") (National Institute for Nuclear Physics) in which there is a [cyclotron](/wiki/Cyclotron "Cyclotron") that uses [protons](/wiki/Proton "Proton") both for [nuclear physics](/wiki/Nuclear_physics "Nuclear physics") experiments and for particle therapy to treat cancer ([proton therapy](/wiki/Proton_therapy "Proton therapy")).[[186]](#cite_note-188)[[187]](#cite_note-189) [Noto](/wiki/Noto "Noto") has one of the largest [radio telescopes](/wiki/Radio_telescope "Radio telescope") in Italy that performs geodetic and astronomical observations.[[188]](#cite_note-190) There are [observatories](/wiki/Observatories "Observatories") in [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo") and Catania, managed by the *[Istituto Nazionale di Astrofisica](/wiki/Istituto_Nazionale_di_Astrofisica "Istituto Nazionale di Astrofisica")* (National Institute for Astrophysics). In the *Observatory of Palermo* the astronomer [Giuseppe Piazzi](/wiki/Giuseppe_Piazzi "Giuseppe Piazzi") discovered the first and the largest [asteroid](/wiki/Asteroid "Asteroid") to be identified [Ceres](/wiki/Ceres_(dwarf_planet) "Ceres (dwarf planet)") (today considered a [dwarf planet](/wiki/Dwarf_planet "Dwarf planet")) on 1 January 1801;[[189]](#cite_note-191) Catania has two observatories, one of which is situated on [Mount Etna](/wiki/Mount_Etna "Mount Etna") at 1,800 metres (5,900 [feet](/wiki/Foot_(unit) "Foot (unit)")).[[190]](#cite_note-192)

[Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_Sicily "Syracuse, Sicily") is also an experimental centre for solar technologies through the creation of the project [Archimede solar power plant](/wiki/Archimede_solar_power_plant "Archimede solar power plant") that is the first [concentrated solar power plant](/wiki/Concentrated_solar_power "Concentrated solar power") to use [molten salt](/wiki/Molten_salt "Molten salt") for heat transfer and storage which is integrated with a [combined-cycle](/wiki/Combined-cycle "Combined-cycle") gas facility. All the plant is owned and operated by [Enel](/wiki/Enel "Enel").[[191]](#cite_note-193)[[192]](#cite_note-194) The touristic town of [Erice](/wiki/Erice "Erice") is also an important science place thanks to the [Ettore Majorana Foundation and Centre for Scientific Culture](/wiki/Ettore_Majorana_Foundation_and_Centre_for_Scientific_Culture "Ettore Majorana Foundation and Centre for Scientific Culture") which
embraces 123 schools from all over the world, covering all branches of science, offering courses, seminars, workshops, and annual meetings. It was founded by the physicist [Antonino Zichichi](/wiki/Antonino_Zichichi "Antonino Zichichi") in honour of another scientist of the island, [Ettore Majorana](/wiki/Ettore_Majorana "Ettore Majorana") known for the [Majorana equation](/wiki/Majorana_equation "Majorana equation") and [Majorana fermions](/wiki/Majorana_fermion "Majorana fermion").[[193]](#cite_note-195) Sicily's famous scientists also include [Stanislao Cannizzaro](/wiki/Stanislao_Cannizzaro "Stanislao Cannizzaro") (chemist), [Giovanni Battista Hodierna](/wiki/Giovanni_Battista_Hodierna "Giovanni Battista Hodierna") and [Niccolò Cacciatore](/wiki/Niccol%C3%B2_Cacciatore "Niccolò Cacciatore") (astronomers).

### Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Facolt%C3%A0_di_Scienze_Politiche_-_Messina.jpg/250px-Facolt%C3%A0_di_Scienze_Politiche_-_Messina.jpg)](/wiki/File:Facolt%C3%A0_di_Scienze_Politiche_-_Messina.jpg)

The [University of Messina](/wiki/University_of_Messina "University of Messina")

Sicily has four universities:

* The [University of Catania](/wiki/University_of_Catania "University of Catania") dates back to 1434 and it is the oldest university in Sicily. It currently hosts 12 faculties and over 62,000 students and it offers undergraduate and postgraduate programs. [Catania](/wiki/Catania "Catania") hosts also the *[Scuola Superiore](/wiki/Scuola_superiore_di_Catania "Scuola superiore di Catania")*, an [academic institution](/wiki/Academic_institution "Academic institution") linked to the University of Catania, aiming for excellence in education.[[194]](#cite_note-196)
* The [University of Palermo](/wiki/University_of_Palermo "University of Palermo") is the island's second-oldest university. It was officially founded in 1806, although historical records indicate that medicine and law have been taught there since the late 15th century. The [Orto botanico di Palermo](/wiki/Orto_botanico_di_Palermo "Orto botanico di Palermo") (Palermo botanical gardens) is home to the university's Department of Botany and is also open to visitors.
* The [University of Messina](/wiki/University_of_Messina "University of Messina"), founded in 1548 by [Ignatius of Loyola](/wiki/Ignatius_of_Loyola "Ignatius of Loyola"). It is organized in 11 Faculties.
* The [Kore University of Enna](/wiki/Kore_University_of_Enna "Kore University of Enna") founded in 1995, is the latest Sicilian university and the first university founded in Sicily after the [Italian Unification](/wiki/Italian_Unification "Italian Unification").

### Cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: Cuisine")]

Main article: [Sicilian cuisine](/wiki/Sicilian_cuisine "Sicilian cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Pasta_alla_Norma_%282563876877%29.jpg/250px-Pasta_alla_Norma_%282563876877%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pasta_alla_Norma_(2563876877).jpg)

*[Pasta alla Norma](/wiki/Pasta_alla_Norma "Pasta alla Norma")* is amongst Sicily's most historic and iconic dishes.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Cannoli_siciliani_%287472226896%29.jpg/250px-Cannoli_siciliani_%287472226896%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cannoli_siciliani_(7472226896).jpg)

[Cannoli](/wiki/Cannoli "Cannoli") with pistachio, candied fruit, and chocolate chips

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Arancine_in_Favignana.jpg/250px-Arancine_in_Favignana.jpg)](/wiki/File:Arancine_in_Favignana.jpg)

[Arancini](/wiki/Arancini "Arancini"), rice balls fried in breadcrumbs

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Sfincione_palermitano.jpg/250px-Sfincione_palermitano.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sfincione_palermitano.jpg)

[Sicilian pizza](/wiki/Sicilian_pizza "Sicilian pizza")

[Sicilian cuisine](/wiki/Sicilian_cuisine "Sicilian cuisine") is the style of cooking on the island of Sicily. It shows traces of all cultures that have existed on the island of Sicily over the last two millennia.[[195]](#cite_note-197) Although its cuisine has much in common with [Italian cuisine](/wiki/Italian_cuisine "Italian cuisine"), Sicilian food also has [Greek](/wiki/Greek_cuisine "Greek cuisine"), [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_cuisine "Spanish cuisine"), [Jewish](/wiki/Jewish_cuisine "Jewish cuisine"), [Maghrebi](/wiki/Maghrebi_cuisine "Maghrebi cuisine"), and [Arab](/wiki/Arab_cuisine "Arab cuisine") influences.[[196]](#cite_note-198) The Sicilian cook [Mithaecus](/wiki/Mithaecus "Mithaecus"), born during 5th century BC, is credited with having brought knowledge of Sicilian gastronomy to [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece"):[[197]](#cite_note-199) his cookbook was the first in Greek, therefore he was the earliest cookbook author in any language whose name is known.

Much of the island was initially [settled by Greek colonists](/wiki/Magna_Graecia "Magna Graecia"), who left a preference for [fish](/wiki/Fish_as_food "Fish as food"), [wheat](/wiki/Wheat "Wheat"), [olives](/wiki/Olive "Olive"), [grapes](/wiki/Vitis_vinifera "Vitis vinifera"), [broad beans](/wiki/Vicia_faba "Vicia faba"), [chickpeas](/wiki/Chickpea "Chickpea"), [lentils](/wiki/Lentil "Lentil"), [almonds](/wiki/Almond "Almond"), [pistachios](/wiki/Pistachio "Pistachio"), and fresh vegetables.
[Arab](/wiki/Arabs "Arabs") influences on Sicilian cuisine trace to the [Arab domination of Sicily](/wiki/Emirate_of_Sicily "Emirate of Sicily") in the 10th and early 11th centuries,[[198]](#cite_note-Piras,_423-200) and include the use of [sugar](/wiki/Sugar "Sugar"), [citrus](/wiki/Citrus "Citrus"), [rice](/wiki/Rice "Rice"), [raisins](/wiki/Raisin "Raisin"), [pine nuts](/wiki/Pine_nut "Pine nut") and spices such as [saffron](/wiki/Saffron "Saffron"), [nutmeg](/wiki/Nutmeg "Nutmeg"), and [cinnamon](/wiki/Cinnamon "Cinnamon"). [Norman](/wiki/Normans "Normans") influences are also found, such as in the fondness for [meat](/wiki/Meat "Meat") dishes.[[199]](#cite_note-201) The [Jewish](/wiki/Jews "Jews") community, who lived in the island, also left their mark on the Sicilian cuisine, they were responsible for introducing garlic fried in olive oil into the sauce.[[200]](#cite_note-202) Later, the [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_cuisine "Spanish cuisine") introduced [numerous items from the New World](/wiki/Columbian_exchange "Columbian exchange"), including [cocoa](/wiki/Chocolate "Chocolate"), [maize](/wiki/Maize "Maize"), [peppers](/wiki/Bell_pepper "Bell pepper"), [zucchini](/wiki/Zucchini "Zucchini"), [potatoes](/wiki/Potato "Potato"), and [tomatoes](/wiki/Tomato "Tomato"), along with other [produce](/wiki/Produce "Produce").[[198]](#cite_note-Piras,_423-200) Much of the island's cuisine encourages the use of fresh vegetables such as [eggplant](/wiki/Eggplant "Eggplant"), [artichoke](/wiki/Artichoke "Artichoke"), and tomatoes, and fish such as [tuna](/wiki/Tuna_as_food "Tuna as food"), [sea bream](/wiki/Sparidae "Sparidae"), [sea bass](/wiki/European_seabass "European seabass"), [cuttlefish](/wiki/Cuttlefish "Cuttlefish"), and [swordfish](/wiki/Swordfish "Swordfish"). In [Trapani](/wiki/Trapani "Trapani") in the extreme western corner of the island, [North African](/wiki/Maghrebi_cuisine "Maghrebi cuisine") influences are clear in the use of [couscous](/wiki/Couscous "Couscous").

The island has a long history of producing a variety of noted cuisines and wines, to the extent that Sicily is sometimes nicknamed *God's Kitchen* because of this.[[201]](#cite_note-203) Every part of Sicily has its speciality (e.g. Cassata is typical of Palermo although available everywhere in Sicily, as is Granita). The ingredients are typically rich in taste while remaining affordable to the general public.[[202]](#cite_note-204) The savoury dishes of Sicily are viewed to be [healthy](/wiki/Healthy_diet "Healthy diet"), using fresh vegetables and fruits, such as tomatoes, [artichokes](/wiki/Artichoke "Artichoke"), [olives](/wiki/Olive "Olive") (including [olive oil](/wiki/Olive_oil "Olive oil")), [citrus](/wiki/Citrus "Citrus"), [apricots](/wiki/Apricot "Apricot"), [aubergines](/wiki/Aubergines "Aubergines"), onions, [beans](/wiki/Bean "Bean"), [raisins](/wiki/Raisin "Raisin") commonly coupled with seafood, freshly caught from the surrounding coastlines, including [tuna](/wiki/Tuna "Tuna"), [sea bream](/wiki/Sea_bream "Sea bream"), [sea bass](/wiki/European_seabass "European seabass"), [cuttlefish](/wiki/Cuttlefish "Cuttlefish"), [swordfish](/wiki/Swordfish "Swordfish"), [sardines](/wiki/Sardine "Sardine"), and others.[[203]](#cite_note-205)

The most well-known part of Sicilian cuisine is the rich sweet dishes including [ice creams](/wiki/Ice_cream "Ice cream") and [pastries](/wiki/Pastry "Pastry"). [Cannoli](/wiki/Cannoli "Cannoli") (singular: *cannolo*), a tube-shaped shell of fried pastry dough filled with a sweet filling usually containing [ricotta](/wiki/Ricotta "Ricotta"), is strongly associated with Sicily worldwide.[[204]](#cite_note-authentic-206) Biancomangiare, biscotti ennesi (cookies native to [Enna](/wiki/Enna "Enna")), braccilatte (a Sicilian version of [doughnuts](/wiki/Doughnut "Doughnut")), [buccellato](/wiki/Buccellato "Buccellato"), [ciarduna](/wiki/Ciarduna "Ciarduna"), [pignoli](/wiki/Pignolo_(macaroon) "Pignolo (macaroon)"), [Biscotti Regina](/wiki/Biscotti_Regina "Biscotti Regina"), [giurgiulena](/wiki/Giurgiulena "Giurgiulena"), [frutta martorana](/wiki/Frutta_martorana "Frutta martorana"), [cassata](/wiki/Cassata "Cassata"), [pignolata](/wiki/Pignolata "Pignolata"), [granita](/wiki/Granita "Granita"), [cuccidati](/wiki/Cuccidati "Cuccidati") (a variety of fig cookie; also known as buccellati) and [cuccìa](/wiki/Cucc%C3%ACa "Cuccìa") are some notable sweet dishes.[[204]](#cite_note-authentic-206)

Like the cuisine of the rest of southern Italy, pasta plays an important part in Sicilian cuisine, as does rice; for example with [arancini](/wiki/Arancini "Arancini").[[205]](#cite_note-207) As well as using some other cheeses, Sicily has spawned some of its own, using both cow's and sheep's milk, such as [pecorino](/wiki/Pecorino_Siciliano "Pecorino Siciliano") and [caciocavallo](/wiki/Caciocavallo "Caciocavallo").[[206]](#cite_note-208) Spices used include [saffron](/wiki/Saffron "Saffron"), [nutmeg](/wiki/Nutmeg "Nutmeg"), [clove](/wiki/Clove "Clove"), [pepper](/wiki/Black_pepper "Black pepper"), and [cinnamon](/wiki/Cinnamon "Cinnamon"), which were introduced by the Arabs. [Parsley](/wiki/Parsley "Parsley") is used abundantly in many dishes. Although Sicilian cuisine is commonly associated with sea food, meat dishes, including [goose](/wiki/Goose "Goose"), [lamb](/wiki/Domestic_sheep "Domestic sheep"), goat, rabbit, and [turkey](/wiki/Turkey_meat "Turkey meat"), are also found in Sicily. It was the [Normans](/wiki/Normans "Normans") and [Swabians](/wiki/Hohenstaufen "Hohenstaufen") who first introduced a fondness for meat dishes to the island.[[207]](#cite_note-209) Some varieties of wine are produced from vines that are relatively unique to the island, such as the [Nero d'Avola](/wiki/Nero_d%27Avola "Nero d'Avola") made near the baroque town of [Noto](/wiki/Noto "Noto").[[208]](#cite_note-210)

### Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=55 "Edit section: Sports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Giuseppe_Gibilisco_Berlin_2009.JPG/250px-Giuseppe_Gibilisco_Berlin_2009.JPG)](/wiki/File:Giuseppe_Gibilisco_Berlin_2009.JPG)

[Giuseppe Gibilisco](/wiki/Giuseppe_Gibilisco "Giuseppe Gibilisco"), [pole vaulter](/wiki/Pole_vaulting "Pole vaulting") from [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_Sicily "Syracuse, Sicily"), [2003 World Champion](/wiki/2003_World_Championships_in_Athletics "2003 World Championships in Athletics") and bronze [Olympic](/wiki/Athletics_at_the_2004_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_pole_vault "Athletics at the 2004 Summer Olympics – Men's pole vault") medalist

The most popular sport in Sicily is [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"), which came to the fore in the late 19th century under the influence of the English. Some of the oldest football clubs in Italy are from Sicily: the three most successful are [Palermo](/wiki/U.S._Citt%C3%A0_di_Palermo "U.S. Città di Palermo"), [Catania](/wiki/Calcio_Catania "Calcio Catania"), and [Messina](/wiki/A.C.R._Messina "A.C.R. Messina"), which have played 29, 17 and 5 seasons in the [Serie A](/wiki/Serie_A "Serie A") respectively. No club from Sicily has ever won Serie A, but football is still deeply embedded in local culture and all over Sicily most towns have a representative team.[[209]](#cite_note-derbysicil-211)

Palermo and Catania have a heated rivalry and compete in the [Sicilian derby](/wiki/Derby_di_Sicilia "Derby di Sicilia") together. Palermo is the only team in Sicily to have played on the European stage, in the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup "UEFA Cup"). In the island, the most noted footballer is [Salvatore Schillaci](/wiki/Salvatore_Schillaci "Salvatore Schillaci"), who won the [Golden Boot](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup_awards "FIFA World Cup awards") at the [1990 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1990_FIFA_World_Cup "1990 FIFA World Cup") with [Italy](/wiki/Italy_national_football_team "Italy national football team").[[209]](#cite_note-derbysicil-211) Other noted players include [Giuseppe Furino](/wiki/Giuseppe_Furino "Giuseppe Furino"), [Pietro Anastasi](/wiki/Pietro_Anastasi "Pietro Anastasi"), [Francesco Coco](/wiki/Francesco_Coco "Francesco Coco"), [Christian Riganò](/wiki/Christian_Rigan%C3%B2 "Christian Riganò"), and [Roberto Galia](/wiki/Roberto_Galia "Roberto Galia").[[209]](#cite_note-derbysicil-211) There have also been some noted managers from the island, such as [Carmelo Di Bella](/wiki/Carmelo_Di_Bella "Carmelo Di Bella") and [Franco Scoglio](/wiki/Franco_Scoglio "Franco Scoglio").

Although football is the most popular sport in Sicily, the island also has participants in other fields. [Amatori Catania](/wiki/Amatori_Catania "Amatori Catania") have competed in the top Italian national [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") league called [National Championship of Excellence](/wiki/Top12 "Top12"). They have even participated at the European level in the [European Challenge Cup](/wiki/European_Challenge_Cup "European Challenge Cup"). Competing in the basketball variation of [Serie A](/wiki/Serie_A_(basketball) "Serie A (basketball)") is [Orlandina Basket](/wiki/Orlandina_Basket "Orlandina Basket") from [Capo d'Orlando](/wiki/Capo_d%27Orlando "Capo d'Orlando") in the [province of Messina](/wiki/Province_of_Messina "Province of Messina"), where the sport has a reasonable following. Various other sports that are played to some extent include volleyball, [handball](/wiki/Team_handball "Team handball"), and [water polo](/wiki/Water_polo "Water polo"). Previously, in [motorsport](/wiki/Motorsport "Motorsport"), Sicily held the prominent [Targa Florio](/wiki/Targa_Florio "Targa Florio") sports car race that took place in the Madonie Mountains, with the start-finish line in [Cerda](/wiki/Cerda "Cerda").[[210]](#cite_note-targa-212) The event was started in 1906 by Sicilian industrialist and automobile enthusiast [Vincenzo Florio](/wiki/Vincenzo_Florio "Vincenzo Florio"), and ran until it was canceled due to safety concerns in 1977.[[210]](#cite_note-targa-212)

From 28 September to 9 October 2005 [Trapani](/wiki/Trapani "Trapani") was the location of Acts 8 and 9 of the [Louis Vuitton Cup](/wiki/Louis_Vuitton_Cup "Louis Vuitton Cup"). This sailing race featured, among other entrants, all boats that took part in the 2007 [America's Cup](/wiki/America%27s_Cup "America's Cup").

### Popular culture and Traditional items

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=56 "Edit section: Popular culture and Traditional items")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/PupiSiciliani.jpg/330px-PupiSiciliani.jpg)](/wiki/File:PupiSiciliani.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Carnival_in_Acireale.jpg/250px-Carnival_in_Acireale.jpg)](/wiki/File:Carnival_in_Acireale.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/A_Nuttata_Ri_Misteri_%21.jpg/250px-A_Nuttata_Ri_Misteri_%21.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_Nuttata_Ri_Misteri_!.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Itali%C3%AB%2C_tafereel_met_ezel_en_wagen%2C_RP-F-F17074.jpg/330px-Itali%C3%AB%2C_tafereel_met_ezel_en_wagen%2C_RP-F-F17074.jpg)](/wiki/File:Itali%C3%AB,_tafereel_met_ezel_en_wagen,_RP-F-F17074.jpg)

**Clockwise from top**:

* The [marionettes](/wiki/Marionettes "Marionettes") used in the [Opera dei Pupi](/wiki/Opera_dei_Pupi "Opera dei Pupi")
* The "[Misteri](/wiki/Misteri_di_Trapani "Misteri di Trapani")", the [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week") procession in [Trapani](/wiki/Trapani "Trapani")
* A traditional [Sicilian cart](/wiki/Sicilian_cart "Sicilian cart"), ca. 1860-1900
* The [carnival](/wiki/Carnival "Carnival") of [Acireale](/wiki/Acireale "Acireale")

Each town and city has its own patron saint, and the [feast days](/wiki/Feast_day "Feast day") are marked by colourful processions through the streets with marching bands and displays of fireworks.

Sicilian religious festivals also include the *presepe vivente* (living [nativity scene](/wiki/Nativity_scene "Nativity scene")), which takes place at Christmas time. Deftly combining religion and folklore, it is a constructed mock 19th-century Sicilian village, complete with a nativity scene, and has people of all ages dressed in the costumes of the period, some impersonating the Holy Family, and others working as artisans of their particular assigned trade. It is normally concluded on [Epiphany](/wiki/Epiphany_(holiday) "Epiphany (holiday)"), often highlighted by the arrival of the [magi](/wiki/Magi "Magi") on horseback.

Oral tradition plays a large role in Sicilian folklore. Many stories passed down from generation to generation involve a character named "[Giufà](/wiki/Giuf%C3%A0 "Giufà")". Anecdotes from this character's life preserve Sicilian culture as well as convey moral messages.

Sicilians also enjoy outdoor festivals, held in the local square or *piazza* where live music and dancing are performed on stage, and food fairs or *sagre* are set up in booths lining the square. These offer various local specialties, as well as typical Sicilian food. Normally these events are concluded with fireworks. A noted *sagra* is the *Sagra del Carciofo* or *[Artichoke](/wiki/Artichoke "Artichoke") Festival*, which is held annually in [Ramacca](/wiki/Ramacca "Ramacca") in April. The most important traditional event in Sicily is the [carnival](/wiki/Carnival "Carnival"). Famous carnivals are in [Acireale](/wiki/Acireale "Acireale"), [Misterbianco](/wiki/Misterbianco "Misterbianco"), [Regalbuto](/wiki/Regalbuto "Regalbuto"), [Paternò](/wiki/Patern%C3%B2 "Paternò"), [Sciacca](/wiki/Sciacca "Sciacca"), [Termini Imerese](/wiki/Termini_Imerese "Termini Imerese").

The **[Opera dei Pupi](/wiki/Opera_dei_Pupi "Opera dei Pupi")** (Opera of the Puppets; [Sicilian](/wiki/Sicilian_language "Sicilian language"): Òpira dî pupi) is a [marionette](/wiki/Marionette "Marionette") theatrical representation of Frankish romantic poems such as the [Song of Roland](/wiki/Song_of_Roland "Song of Roland") or *[Orlando furioso](/wiki/Orlando_furioso "Orlando furioso")* that is one of the characteristic cultural traditions of Sicily. The sides of donkey carts are decorated with intricate, painted scenes; these same tales are enacted in traditional [puppet theatres](/wiki/Puppet_theatre "Puppet theatre") featuring hand-made marionettes of wood. The opera of the puppets and the Sicilian tradition of *cantastorî* (singers of tales) are rooted in the Provençal [troubadour](/wiki/Troubadour "Troubadour") tradition in Sicily during the reign of [Frederick II, Holy Roman Emperor](/wiki/Frederick_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Frederick II, Holy Roman Emperor"), in the first half of the 13th century. A great place to see this marionette art is the puppet theatres of [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo"). The Sicilian marionette theatre Opera dei Pupi was proclaimed in 2001 and inscribed in 2008 in the [UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage Lists](/wiki/UNESCO_Intangible_Cultural_Heritage_Lists "UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage Lists").[[211]](#cite_note-213)

Today, there are only a few troupes that maintain the tradition. They often perform for tourists. However, there are no longer the great historical families of marionettists, such as the Greco of [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo"); the [Canino](/wiki/Gaspare_Canino "Gaspare Canino") of [Partinico](/wiki/Partinico "Partinico") and [Alcamo](/wiki/Alcamo "Alcamo"); Crimi, Trombetta and Napoli of [Catania](/wiki/Catania "Catania"), Pennisi and Macri of [Acireale](/wiki/Acireale "Acireale"), Profeta of [Licata](/wiki/Licata "Licata"), Gargano and Grasso of [Agrigento](/wiki/Agrigento "Agrigento"). One can, however, admire the richest collection of marionettes at the Museo Internazionale delle Marionette Antonio Pasqualino and at the [Museo Etnografico Siciliano Giuseppe Pitrè](/wiki/Museo_Etnografico_Siciliano_Giuseppe_Pitr%C3%A8 "Museo Etnografico Siciliano Giuseppe Pitrè") in Palermo. Other elaborate marionettes are on display at the Museo Civico Vagliasindi in [Randazzo](/wiki/Randazzo "Randazzo").

The [Sicilian cart](/wiki/Sicilian_cart "Sicilian cart") is an ornate, colourful style of a horse- or donkey-drawn cart native to Sicily. Sicilian woodcarver [George Petralia](/w/index.php?title=George_Petralia&action=edit&redlink=1 "George Petralia (page does not exist)") states that horses were mostly used in the city and flat plains, while donkeys or mules were more often used in rough terrain for hauling heavy loads.[[212]](#cite_note-214) The cart has two wheels and is primarily handmade out of wood with iron components.

The Sicilian [coppola](/wiki/Coppola_(cap) "Coppola (cap)") is a traditional kind of [flat cap](/wiki/Flat_cap "Flat cap") typically worn by men in Sicily. First used by English nobles during the late 18th century, the *tascu* began being used in Sicily in the early 20th century as a [driving cap](/wiki/Flat_cap "Flat cap"), usually worn by car drivers. The *coppola* is usually made in [tweed](/wiki/Tweed_(cloth) "Tweed (cloth)"). Today it is widely regarded as a definitive symbol of Sicilian heritage.[[213]](#cite_note-215)

### Flag and emblem

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sicily&action=edit&section=57 "Edit section: Flag and emblem")]

Main articles: [Triskelion § Sicily](/wiki/Triskelion#Sicily "Triskelion"), and [Flag of Sicily](/wiki/Flag_of_Sicily "Flag of Sicily")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/The_triskelion_symbol_of_Sicily.jpg/330px-The_triskelion_symbol_of_Sicily.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_triskelion_symbol_of_Sicily.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Dinos_with_Triskelos%2C_pottery%2C_Gela%2C_620-600_BC%2C_AM_Agrigento%2C_121019.jpg/250px-Dinos_with_Triskelos%2C_pottery%2C_Gela%2C_620-600_BC%2C_AM_Agrigento%2C_121019.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dinos_with_Triskelos,_pottery,_Gela,_620-600_BC,_AM_Agrigento,_121019.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Red_figure_lekythos%2C_470_BC%2C_warrior%2C_AM_Syracuse%2C_121431.jpg/120px-Red_figure_lekythos%2C_470_BC%2C_warrior%2C_AM_Syracuse%2C_121431.jpg)](/wiki/File:Red_figure_lekythos,_470_BC,_warrior,_AM_Syracuse,_121431.jpg)

**Clockwise from top**:

* The [Triskelion](/wiki/Triskelion "Triskelion") and [Gorgoneion](/wiki/Gorgoneion "Gorgoneion") symbol of Sicily
* Red-figure [Lekythos](/wiki/Lekythos "Lekythos") from a tomb near [Licata](/wiki/Licata "Licata"), ca. 470 BCE, depicting a warrior with a triskelion on the shield
* [Triskelion](/wiki/Triskelion "Triskelion") painted on [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_art "Ancient Greek art") of [Magna Graecia](/wiki/Magna_Graecia "Magna Graecia") vase, [Agrigento](/wiki/Agrigento "Agrigento")

The [Flag of Sicily](/wiki/Flag_of_Sicily "Flag of Sicily"), regarded as a regional icon, was first adopted in 1282, after the [Sicilian Vespers](/wiki/Sicilian_Vespers "Sicilian Vespers") of [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo").[[214]](#cite_note-216) It is characterised by the presence of the [triskeles](/wiki/Triskelion "Triskelion") in the middle, depicting the head of [Medusa](/wiki/Medusa "Medusa") and three wheat ears representing the extreme fertility of the land of Sicily. In early mythology, when Medusa was slain and beheaded by Perseus, the Medusa head was placed in the centre of Athena's shield.[[215]](#cite_note-217)

Palermo and Corleone were the first two cities to found a confederation against the [Angevin](/wiki/Capetian_House_of_Anjou "Capetian House of Anjou") rule. The triskeles symbol came to be on the Sicilian flag in 1943 during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") when [Andrea Finocchiaro Aprile](/wiki/Andrea_Finocchiaro_Aprile "Andrea Finocchiaro Aprile") led an independence movement, in collaboration with the allies. Their plan was to help Sicily become independent and form a free republic. The colours, likewise introduced in the 1940s, respectively represent the cities of [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo") and [Corleone](/wiki/Corleone "Corleone"). The separatist behind the movement used a yellow and red flag with the Trinacria in the centre of it. When [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") ended, Sicily was recognized as an autonomous region in the Italian Republic.

The flag became the official public flag of the *Regione Siciliana* in January 2000, after the passing of an apposite regional law, which advocates its use on public buildings, schools and city halls along with the national [Italian flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Italy "Flag of Italy") and the [European](/wiki/Flag_of_EU "Flag of EU") one.

Familiar as an ancient symbol of the region, the [Triskelion](/wiki/Triskelion "Triskelion") is also featured on Greek coins of [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_Sicily "Syracuse, Sicily"), such as coins of [Agathocles](/wiki/Agathocles "Agathocles") (317–289 BC).The symbol dates back to when Sicily was part of [Magna Graecia](/wiki/Magna_Graecia "Magna Graecia"), the colonial extension of [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece") beyond the [Aegean](/wiki/Aegean_Sea "Aegean Sea").[[216]](#cite_note-218) The triskelion was revived, as a [neoclassic](/wiki/Neoclassicism "Neoclassicism") – and non-[Bourbon](/wiki/House_of_Bourbon "House of Bourbon") – emblem for the new Napoleonic Kingdom of the [Two Sicilies](/wiki/Two_Sicilies "Two Sicilies"), by [Joachim Murat](/wiki/Joachim_Murat "Joachim Murat") in 1808. In the case of Sicily, the triskelion symbol is said to represent the three [capes](/wiki/Cape_(geography) "Cape (geography)") ([headlands](/wiki/Headland "Headland") or [promontories](/wiki/Promontory "Promontory") of the island of Sicily, namely: [Pelorus](/wiki/Punta_del_Faro "Punta del Faro") (Peloro, Tip of Faro, Messina: North-East); [Pachynus](/wiki/Capo_Passero "Capo Passero") (Passero, Syracuse: South); and [Lilybæum](/wiki/Marsala "Marsala") (Lilibeo, Cape Boeo, Marsala: West), which form three points of a triangle.[[217]](#cite_note-219)

174. **[^](#cite_ref-175)** "Palazzo" (pl. *palazzi*): is any large building in a town, state or private (often much smaller than the term *palace* implies in the [English-speaking world](/wiki/English-speaking_world "English-speaking world")). While *palazzo* is the technically correct appellation and postal address, no Sicilian aristocrat would ever use the word, instead referring to his or her own house, however large, as "casa". "Palazzo" followed by the family name was the term used by officials, tradesmen, and delivery men. Gefen, p. 15.
